docs-site/docs/proxy-worker.md:The proxy worker is a Cloudflare Worker that sits in front of the production domain and routes traffic between the frontend and the documentation site. docs-site/docs/proxy-worker.md:- `your-domain.com/*` → Astro frontend (Cloudflare Pages) docs-site/docs/proxy-worker.md:- `your-domain.com/docs/*` → Docusaurus documentation site (separate Cloudflare Pages project) docs-site/docs/proxy-worker.md:Set this in the Cloudflare dashboard: **Workers & Pages → proxy-worker → Settings → Variables**. docs-site/docs/proxy-worker.md:This deploys the worker to Cloudflare. Make sure you have `wrangler` configured with the correct Cloudflare account. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:id: cloudflare-setup docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:title: Cloudflare Dashboard Setup docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:sidebar_label: Cloudflare Setup docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:# Cloudflare Dashboard Setup docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:Step-by-step guide for setting up Sommelier Arena through the Cloudflare web UI. Note: Wrangler CLI is required to publish the TypeScript proxy worker (`proxy-worker/index.ts`) because the Cloudflare Dashboard editor cannot build TypeScript or run the project's build step. Keep `wrangler` available locally or in CI (see examples below). docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:Goal: create a Cloudflare KV namespace and bind it to the worker as `HOSTS_KV` so the backend can store cross-session host lists. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:Two equivalent ways: (A) Cloudflare Dashboard (GUI) — easiest; (B) Wrangler CLI — scriptable. Choose one. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:1. Open https://dash.cloudflare.com and sign in to the Cloudflare account that will host the Pages / Workers. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Ensure you are authenticated with Cloudflare locally (`npx wrangler login`) or have a valid `CF_API_TOKEN` stored in GitHub Secrets for CI. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:1. After `npx partykit deploy` succeeds (CI or local), open Cloudflare Dashboard → Workers & Pages → your Worker and confirm the binding `HOSTS_KV` appears under **Settings / Variables & Bindings**. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- If you accidentally created the namespace in a different Cloudflare account, delete and recreate it in the intended account, then update `partykit.json` and re-deploy. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:## 2. Deploy the frontend to Cloudflare Pages docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:2. Authorize Cloudflare to access your GitHub account if prompted docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: - **Production branch**: `cloudflare-migration` docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:To apply `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` to Cloudflare Pages automatically you can either: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: To apply `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` to Cloudflare Pages automatically you can: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: - Use the Cloudflare Pages REST API to set environment variables for the project (requires CF_API_TOKEN and the account + project identifiers). Add a CI step that calls the Pages API to update or create the `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` variable and then trigger a Pages redeploy; or docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:## 3. Deploy the docs to Cloudflare Pages docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:**Cloudflare Pages → sommelier-arena → Custom domains** docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:5. Cloudflare will add the required DNS CNAME automatically (since `ducatillon.net` is already managed on Cloudflare) docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Cloudflare Account ID and Zone ID (for API route creation; optional if using the Dashboard GUI). docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:1. Deploy the Docusaurus docs to Cloudflare Pages (see Section 3 — Deploy the frontend to Cloudflare Pages): docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: - Cloudflare Dashboard → Workers & Pages → Workers → select `sommelier-arena-proxy` → Settings → Variables & Bindings → Add variable: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- If you need to create/update routes from CI, use the Cloudflare REST API (requires `CF_API_TOKEN` and `ZONE_ID`) or manage routes in `wrangler.toml` and let `npx wrangler deploy` apply them. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:Use the Cloudflare REST API to add the `/docs*` route for your zone. Example (replace placeholders): docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- name: Create /docs route (Cloudflare API) docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: curl -s -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:3. Worker logs: Cloudflare Dashboard → Workers → View logs (or use `wrangler tail` with `CF_API_TOKEN` to stream logs locally). docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Cloudflare does not expose a one-click "rollback" for Workers. To rollback: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:There are two repeatable ways to deploy the proxy worker from the repository: (A) Cloudflare Dashboard (manual GUI) — already documented above; (B) Wrangler CLI (scriptable). The Wrangler approach is shown here so deployments can be automated from your machine or CI. docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- A Cloudflare API token (recommended) or global API key with permissions to edit Workers and Routes docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Your Cloudflare Account ID (can be found on the Cloudflare dashboard) docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:1. Authenticate with Cloudflare if not already authenticated: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- After publishing, go to Cloudflare Dashboard → Workers & Pages → your Worker → Triggers and add a route with: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Use the Cloudflare REST API to create a route programmatically. You need: docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md: - CF_ACCOUNT_ID (your Cloudflare account id) docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:ZONE_ID=ducatillon_net_zone_id # find this on Cloudflare dashboard under the domain docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:curl -X GET "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, click `ducatillon.net` in your domain list docs-site/docs/intro.md:Everything runs on **Cloudflare**: the frontend is a static Astro site on Cloudflare Pages; the game backend is a PartyKit Durable Object (Cloudflare Workers); session lists are stored in Cloudflare KV. docs-site/docs/intro.md:| [Deployment](deployment) | Cloudflare Pages + PartyKit deploy guide | docs-site/docs/intro.md:| [Cloudflare Setup](cloudflare-setup) | Step-by-step Cloudflare dashboard walkthrough | docs-site/docs/networking.md:## Production — Cloudflare docs-site/docs/networking.md:In production the PartyKit server is deployed to Cloudflare Workers via `npx partykit deploy`. The game URL becomes: docs-site/docs/networking.md:The Cloudflare Pages frontend is built with: docs-site/docs/networking.md:The browser connects directly to the PartyKit Workers URL — **no nginx proxy** is needed because Cloudflare handles TLS, routing, and WebSocket upgrades natively. docs-site/docs/networking.md:Browser ──wss://sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev/parties/main/1234──► Cloudflare Workers (PartyKit DO) docs-site/docs/networking.md:| Production | `sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev` | Cloudflare (native) | docs-site/docs/networking.md:| `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard (Production) | `.partykit.dev` | docs-site/docs/deployment.md:The production stack runs entirely on Cloudflare's free tier. No servers to manage. docs-site/docs/deployment.md:| Frontend | Cloudflare Pages (`front/`) | Git push → auto-deploy | docs-site/docs/deployment.md:| Backend | PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers) | `npx partykit deploy` | docs-site/docs/deployment.md:| Docs | Cloudflare Pages (`docs-site/`) | Git push → auto-deploy | docs-site/docs/deployment.md:| Proxy Worker | Cloudflare Workers | Deploy via Wrangler (see below) | docs-site/docs/deployment.md:> **First time**: `npx partykit login` to authenticate with your Cloudflare account. docs-site/docs/deployment.md:## Step 2 — Create Cloudflare KV namespace docs-site/docs/deployment.md:In the Cloudflare dashboard: docs-site/docs/deployment.md:## Step 3 — Frontend on Cloudflare Pages docs-site/docs/deployment.md:2. Select repository, branch: `cloudflare-migration` docs-site/docs/deployment.md:## Step 4 — Docs on Cloudflare Pages docs-site/docs/deployment.md:In Cloudflare Pages → your front project → **Custom domains** → Add domain → `sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net` docs-site/docs/deployment.md:- The Cloudflare Dashboard editor cannot build or publish TypeScript worker projects. To publish the repo's TypeScript worker (`proxy-worker/index.ts`) use Wrangler. docs-site/docs/deployment.md: - Cloudflare Dashboard → Workers & Pages → Workers → open `sommelier-arena-proxy` → Triggers → Add route: docs-site/docs/deployment.md:> See [Cloudflare Setup](cloudflare-setup) for a step-by-step dashboard walkthrough with UI labels. docs-site/docs/deployment.md:Cloudflare Pages keeps deployment history. Roll back via dashboard → Deployments → select older deployment → **Rollback to this deployment**. docs-site/docs/testing-and-preview.md:This page documents the recommended local workflow for building and previewing the Docusaurus site as it will be deployed under /docs (Cloudflare Pages parity). docs-site/docs/data-persistence.md:The `GameSession` Durable Object persists all state to built-in SQLite-backed storage. State survives DO eviction (Cloudflare may evict idle DOs after ~30 s of inactivity but restores from storage on next request). docs-site/docs/data-persistence.md:## Cloudflare KV docs-site/docs/data-persistence.md:> **Note**: In `npx partykit dev` mode, DO storage is in-memory only. Production Cloudflare Workers use real SQLite-backed DO storage. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:| **Frontend** | Astro dev server
`http://localhost:4321` | nginx
`http://localhost:4321` | Cloudflare Pages (CDN) | docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:| **PartyKit backend** | `npx partykit dev`
`ws://localhost:1999` | PartyKit container
(internal, nginx proxied) | Cloudflare Workers +
Durable Objects | docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:| **Documentation** | Docusaurus dev
`http://localhost:3002` | Docusaurus container
`http://localhost:3002` | Cloudflare Pages
(separate project) | docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:| **Cloudflare KV** (`HOSTS_KV`) | ❌ Not available | ❌ Not available | ✅ Real KV namespace | docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:## How `npx partykit dev` works — no Cloudflare needed docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:When you run `npm run dev` in `back/` (which runs `npx partykit dev --port 1999`), PartyKit starts a **local HTTP + WebSocket server** that emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:- **No Cloudflare account, no API tokens, no internet connection** is required for local development. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:In production, `room.storage` is backed by SQLite. When a DO is evicted from memory, Cloudflare persists its storage to SQLite and restores it when the DO wakes. The `onStart()` lifecycle hook in `back/game.ts` reads saved state on wake. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:> ⚠️ **Local dev note**: Because Cloudflare KV is not available locally (see below), the Host Dashboard session list is populated **exclusively from localStorage** in local dev. This means session history lives in your browser only — it's not shared across devices or browsers. Use the 🗑 delete button to clean up stale sessions. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:### 3. Cloudflare KV — `HOSTS_KV` — not available locally docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:The backend also writes to a Cloudflare KV namespace (`HOSTS_KV`) to maintain a cross-session index per host. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:**Production**: A real Cloudflare KV namespace (see [Cloudflare Setup](./cloudflare-setup.md) for how to create and bind it). Enables a host to see their sessions when opening the app on a new browser or device, since the server can serve the session list from KV independent of localStorage. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:1. **Astro builds to pure static files** — HTML, CSS, and JavaScript bundles. No Node.js server runs at request time. Cloudflare Pages serves these files from its CDN globally. docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:Browser Cloudflare (production) docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md: │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Pages CDN docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md: │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Workers docs-site/docs/local-vs-prod.md:| Works locally but missing sessions in prod | `HOSTS_KV` binding not configured — see [Cloudflare Setup](./cloudflare-setup.md) | docs-site/docs/architecture.md:│ └── index.ts ← Cloudflare Worker: routes /docs/* to docs Pages docs-site/docs/architecture.md: Cloudflare KV docs-site/docs/quick-start.md::::tip No Cloudflare account needed for local dev docs-site/docs/quick-start.md:`npx partykit dev` runs a **local in-memory simulator** — it emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine with no internet connection required. Note: Cloudflare KV (`HOSTS_KV`) is not available locally; session history comes from browser localStorage only. See [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) for the full comparison. docs-site/docs/quick-start.md:This repository standardizes on building the docs for deployment under `/docs` (Cloudflare Pages). To preview the site exactly as production will serve it: docs-site/docs/quick-start.md:| `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard | `sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev` (prod) | docs-site/docs/tech-stack.md:| Backend | **PartyKit** (Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects) | One DO instance per game session | docs-site/docs/tech-stack.md:| Session index | **Cloudflare KV** (`SOMMELIER_HOSTS` namespace) | Maps `host:{id}` → list of session codes | docs-site/docs/tech-stack.md:| DNS + TLS | **Cloudflare** (`ducatillon.net`) | Managed; zero cert renewal | docs-site/docs/tech-stack.md:| Docs proxy | **Cloudflare Worker** (`proxy-worker/index.ts`) | Routes `/docs/*` to Docusaurus Pages project | docs-site/docs/tech-stack.md:2. **€0/month** — Cloudflare free tier covers all traffic for a casual dinner-party app.

  - user-stories.md

2. Update internal cross-links: several pages (intro.md, local-vs-prod.md, networking.md) link to the existing files. After merge, update links to point to the canonical doc.
3. Add frontmatter tags (audience: developer|user|partner|ai) and short summaries to pages to enable persona landing pages and machine-readable index.
4. Add a machine-readable index (generated later) that maps path→title→audience→tags.
5. Add CI: run `npm ci` and `npm run build` as part of docs CI; add a link-checking step and markdown lint.

- Inventory of docs created (listed above).
- Overlap detection flagged the three deployment-related files for merge.

Suggested immediate actions:
- Create canonical merged doc and update cross-links.
- Generate persona landing pages and update sidebars to expose them.
- Add frontmatter to each doc (scriptable).
- Add CI steps for docs validation.

(End of report)

data.reasoningText:
**Parsing build output**

We successfully ran `npm ci` and the build exited with code 0, so that’s great! Next, I want to check for broken links since Docusaurus may report warnings. I need to inspect the build logs, perhaps using bash to grep the temporary file for any "warning" or "error."

I'll also run a quick validation report to identify duplicates and overlapping content in files like `deployment.md`, `quick-start.md`, and `cloudflare-setup.md`. Merging these could simplify our documentation!**Updating SQL for reports**

I need to update SQL to insert a new todo for the validation report after completing the discovery audit. Once that's marked as done, I need to decide where to create the validation report file. Should I place it in the session-state or directly in the repo under `docs-site/docs/validation-report.md`? While it's often better to have it in the repo for visibility, I’ll go ahead and create the report in session-state for now, making sure to have an absolute path.**Creating validation report**

The repo's docs folder exists, so I can create the validation report file summarizing the results like build success, duplicates found, and recommendations for merging files with redirects. I’ll use `functions.create` to create this file, ensuring the path `docs-site/docs/validation-report.md` doesn't already exist. I also need to update SQL to mark the discovery audit as done using `functions.sql`. I’ve already called `report_intent` earlier, which means I can proceed with additional tool calls in this turn without repeating that step.**Executing further tool calls**

I need to make additional tool calls, like grepping the build log, creating the report file, and updating SQL. Ideally, I’d bundle these into one parallel call. Since we’ve already used tools in this turn, I don’t need to call `report_intent` again now. We can still call more tools in the same message. So, I’ll execute `functions.bash`, `functions.create`, and `functions.sql` in parallel using `multi_tool_use.parallel` to keep things efficient.**Executing parallel tool calls**

I want to run a bash command to grep for "WARN" or "ERROR" in the build log file, then create the validation report and update the todo status to done. I can make a parallel call using `functions.bash` to get the relevant warnings and errors, `functions.create` to create the validation report, and `functions.sql` to update the discovery audit status. I’ll ensure to use the correct file path and create the report at the absolute path specified. Finally, I’ll execute this in parallel!

**Updating documentation structure**

I need to update references for [Deployment] to [Deployment & Deploy], and that involves editing multiple files. Instead of modifying all 19 files manually, it makes sense to at least add frontmatter tags to the merged and deprecated files. I can generate a machine-readable index JSON file by scanning the files. I’ll create this as `docs-site/docs/docs-index.json`.

I’ll also create persona landing pages and consider updates to the sidebar config, which may be more invasive, but let’s at least get the persona pages done. Plus, I need to edit the top of certain files to add deprecation notices.**Updating documentation with redirects**

Docusaurus supports frontmatter 'redirect_from', but I could also just add a note at the top referencing the new document and possibly include 'slug'. To be safer, I think I’ll add a banner that says, "This page is deprecated; see ...".

I should update todo statuses: mark 'merge-deployment-docs' as in progress and ensure 'validation-checks' is accounted for. The next steps involve creating a new file with merged content, incorporating information from existing documents, and editing the original deployment.md to replace its content with a deprecation notice while ensuring the old strings match exactly.**Deciding on file edits**

I’m considering how to replace the top lines of the file, starting with " ---\nid: deployment...". It might be safer to replace the header section and the first lines, but I could also just create a new file named 'deployment-deprecated.md' and then edit the links accordingly.

They want to maintain originals while deprecating them, so an easy way could be to add a deprecation note at the top. I’ll need to ensure the edits match the exact old strings, particularly for the first six lines of deployment.md.**Preparing frontmatter updates**

I can set the old strings to the first three lines of '---\nid: deployment\ntitle: Deployment' and replace them with new frontmatter plus a deprecation message while preserving the existing frontmatter structure.

I'll create a new content string to replace the start, ensuring it matches the existing top exactly. In deployment.md, I noted that lines 1-5 need to be replaced, adding an updated frontmatter that includes 'deprecated: true' along with a top message. 

I'll follow a similar approach for quick-start.md and cloudflare-setup.md, adjusting their initial lines accordingly.**Creating persona pages and managing status updates**

I need to create several persona pages: for-developers.md, for-product.md, for-partners.md, for-automation.md, and get-started.md. Each will have frontmatter with id, title, sidebar label, and links to relevant documents, including audience tags.

Next, I’ll build a docs-index.json file that maps titles to audience tags using our earlier inventory. I need to update todo tasks, marking 'merge-deployment-docs' and 'audience-entrypoints' as done. 

I also need to prepare tool calls, ensuring to include functions.report_intent first, followed by functions.create, functions.edit, and functions.sql to update statuses. Finally, I’ll craft content for the new files, making sure any edits have exact old_str matching.

arguments.file_text:
--- id: deployment-and-deploy title: Deployment & Deploy sidebar_label: Deployment audience: developer tags: [deployment, cloudflare, partykit, pages, wrangler] --- # Deployment & Deploy This canonical document consolidates deployment and Cloudflare setup steps for Sommelier Arena. It replaces `deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, and the deployment sections of `quick-start.md`. Note: Use the Wrangler CLI for reproducible, scriptable Worker deployments and Cloudflare Pages API for automation. Manual Dashboard steps are referenced for one-off tasks only. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Browser -->|HTTPS| Pages[Cloudflare Pages
(Frontend)] Browser -->|WSS| PartyKit[PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects)] Pages --> Proxy[Proxy Worker] Proxy --> Docs[Docs (Docusaurus Pages)] PartyKit --> KV[Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV)] PartyKit -->|Durable Object storage| SQLite[SQLite (Worker DO storage)] ``` ## Summary of services - Frontend: Cloudflare Pages (front/) - Backend: PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects) — deployed with `npx partykit deploy` - Docs: Cloudflare Pages (docs-site/) - Proxy Worker: Cloudflare Worker (proxy-worker/index.ts) — deploy with Wrangler ## Recommended automated flow (CI) 1. Build frontend artifact (`npm --prefix front run build`) and upload to Pages via API or Git push. 2. Build docs (`npm --prefix docs-site run build`) and upload to Pages as a separate Pages project. 3. After Pages projects are deployed, capture their pages.dev URLs and publish the proxy worker with `DOCS_ORIGIN` and other env vars injected via Wrangler or the Pages API. ## PartyKit (backend) Deploy: ```bash # From repo root npx partykit login # authenticate (first time) npx partykit deploy ``` Notes: - PartyKit publishes Durable Objects to a project-specific domain: `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev`. - Ensure `partykit.json` contains KV namespace bindings where required. ## Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV) Create a KV namespace and bind it to PartyKit/Worker deployments. Using Wrangler (scriptable): ```bash # Create namespace via Wrangler (requires CF_API_TOKEN) npx wrangler kv:namespace create "SOMMELIER_HOSTS" --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID # Wrangler prints the namespace id: add it to partykit.json bindings ``` ## Cloudflare Pages (Frontend & Docs) Frontend (Pages project) - Root dir: `front` - Build command: `npm run build` - Output dir: `dist` - Env: `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` = `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev` Docs (Pages project) - Root dir: `docs-site` - Build command: `npm run build` - Output dir: `build` or as configured in docusaurus ## Proxy Worker (wrangler) Use Wrangler to build & publish the TypeScript proxy worker (recommended): ```toml # wrangler.toml (example) name = "sommelier-arena-proxy" main = "proxy-worker/index.ts" compatibility_date = "2026-03-24" ``` Publish (example): ```bash npx wrangler whoami # Deploy with DOCS_ORIGIN injected (replace with actual pages.dev domain) npx wrangler deploy --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://sommelier-arena-docs.pages.dev ``` To create a route for `/docs*` programmatically, use the Cloudflare REST API: ```bash curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $CF_API_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ --data '{ "pattern": "sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs*", "script": "sommelier-arena-proxy" }' ``` ## Verification - Visit `https://sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs` and confirm the docs load. - Confirm frontend loads at configured custom domain and connects to PartyKit via `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST`. ## Rollback - Cloudflare Pages: rollback via Pages → Deployments. - Workers: re-deploy previous bundle via Wrangler with the previous commit. ## Notes & CI recommendations - Do not hard-code `DOCS_ORIGIN`; inject via CI or Wrangler `--var`. - Store `CF_API_TOKEN`, `CF_ACCOUNT_ID`, `ZONE_ID` in GitHub Actions secrets and use them in CI steps.

# For Developers

This page is the developer entrypoint. It links to quick start, local vs prod, testing, architecture, and deployment guides.

Useful links:

- [Quick Start](./quick-start) — local development modes (fast dev, docker, docs preview)
- [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) — environment differences
- [Testing & Preview](./testing-and-preview) — how to run unit and E2E tests
- [Architecture](./architecture) — system architecture and diagrams
- [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy) — production deploy, Cloudflare, Wrangler
- [Tech Stack](./tech-stack) — technology overview

If you are automating deployments or writing CI, start with the Deployment & Deploy guide and the examples in `/.github/workflows/`.

---
id: for-product
title: For Product & Users
sidebar_label: For Product & Users
audience: product
tags: [overview, user-guide]
---

# For Product & Users

This page is for product managers and end-users who want a high-level overview and user-facing guides.

Key links:

- [Overview](./overview) — What the project is and how it works
- [Quick Start](./quick-start) — How to run locally for demos
- [Gameplay Workflow](./gameplay-workflow) — How sessions and questions flow
- [Features](./features) — Product feature list
- [User Stories](./user-stories) — Personas and stories

title: For Partners / Investors
sidebar_label: For Partners
audience: partner
tags: [partners, deployment, ops]
---

# For Partners / Investors

High-level operational summary, hosting model, and integration points.

- Hosting: Cloudflare Pages + Workers
- Integration: API endpoints and expected traffic patterns
- Contact: maintainers and SLA expectations

See [Tech Stack](./tech-stack) and [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy) for more details.

---
id: for-automation
title: For Automation / AI
sidebar_label: For Automation/AI
audience: ai
tags: [automation, ai, index]
---

# For Automation / AI

This page provides machine-friendly entry points and links to a generated JSON index of docs, frontmatter conventions, and common tasks an automation agent might perform.

- [Docs index (JSON)](./docs-index.json)
- Frontmatter conventions: `audience`, `tags`, `summary`
- Suggested tasks: find deployment steps, extract env var names, produce config comparison table

Automation agents should prefer canonical docs (e.g., `deployment-and-deploy.md`) and use the JSON index for fast lookups.

audience: all

- [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy)

--- id: deployment-and-deploy title: Deployment & Deploy sidebar_label: Deployment audience: developer tags: [deployment, cloudflare, partykit, pages, wrangler] --- # Deployment & Deploy This canonical document consolidates deployment and Cloudflare setup steps for Sommelier Arena. It replaces `deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, and the deployment sections of `quick-start.md`. Note: Use the Wrangler CLI for reproducible, scriptable Worker deployments and Cloudflare Pages API for automation. Manual Dashboard steps are referenced for one-off tasks only. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Browser -->|HTTPS| Pages[Cloudflare Pages
(Frontend)] Browser -->|WSS| PartyKit[PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects)] Pages --> Proxy[Proxy Worker] Proxy --> Docs[Docs (Docusaurus Pages)] PartyKit --> KV[Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV)] PartyKit -->|Durable Object storage| SQLite[SQLite (Worker DO storage)] ``` ## Summary of services - Frontend: Cloudflare Pages (front/) - Backend: PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects) — deployed with `npx partykit deploy` - Docs: Cloudflare Pages (docs-site/) - Proxy Worker: Cloudflare Worker (proxy-worker/index.ts) — deploy with Wrangler ## Recommended automated flow (CI) 1. Build frontend artifact (`npm --prefix front run build`) and upload to Pages via API or Git push. 2. Build docs (`npm --prefix docs-site run build`) and upload to Pages as a separate Pages project. 3. After Pages projects are deployed, capture their pages.dev URLs and publish the proxy worker with `DOCS_ORIGIN` and other env vars injected via Wrangler or the Pages API. ## PartyKit (backend) Deploy: ```bash # From repo root npx partykit login # authenticate (first time) npx partykit deploy ``` Notes: - PartyKit publishes Durable Objects to a project-specific domain: `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev`. - Ensure `partykit.json` contains KV namespace bindings where required. ## Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV) Create a KV namespace and bind it to PartyKit/Worker deployments. Using Wrangler (scriptable): ```bash # Create namespace via Wrangler (requires CF_API_TOKEN) npx wrangler kv:namespace create "SOMMELIER_HOSTS" --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID # Wrangler prints the namespace id: add it to partykit.json bindings ``` ## Cloudflare Pages (Frontend & Docs) Frontend (Pages project) - Root dir: `front` - Build command: `npm run build` - Output dir: `dist` - Env: `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` = `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev` Docs (Pages project) - Root dir: `docs-site` - Build command: `npm run build` - Output dir: `build` or as configured in docusaurus ## Proxy Worker (wrangler) Use Wrangler to build & publish the TypeScript proxy worker (recommended): ```toml # wrangler.toml (example) name = "sommelier-arena-proxy" main = "proxy-worker/index.ts" compatibility_date = "2026-03-24" ``` Publish (example): ```bash npx wrangler whoami # Deploy with DOCS_ORIGIN injected (replace with actual pages.dev domain) npx wrangler deploy --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://sommelier-arena-docs.pages.dev ``` To create a route for `/docs*` programmatically, use the Cloudflare REST API: ```bash curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $CF_API_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ --data '{ "pattern": "sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs*", "script": "sommelier-arena-proxy" }' ``` ## Verification - Visit `https://sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs` and confirm the docs load. - Confirm frontend loads at configured custom domain and connects to PartyKit via `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST`. ## Rollback - Cloudflare Pages: rollback via Pages → Deployments. - Workers: re-deploy previous bundle via Wrangler with the previous commit. ## Notes & CI recommendations - Do not hard-code `DOCS_ORIGIN`; inject via CI or Wrangler `--var`. - Store `CF_API_TOKEN`, `CF_ACCOUNT_ID`, `ZONE_ID` in GitHub Actions secrets and use them in CI steps.

toolArgs.file_text:
---
id: for-developers
title: For Developers
sidebar_label: For Developers
audience: developer
tags: [onboarding, dev, setup]
---

# For Developers

This page is the developer entrypoint. It links to quick start, local vs prod, testing, architecture, and deployment guides.

Useful links:

- [Quick Start](./quick-start) — local development modes (fast dev, docker, docs preview)
- [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) — environment differences
- [Testing & Preview](./testing-and-preview) — how to run unit and E2E tests
- [Architecture](./architecture) — system architecture and diagrams
- [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy) — production deploy, Cloudflare, Wrangler

2. id: intro

6. ---
7. 
8. # Sommelier Arena
9. 
10. A real-time blind wine tasting quiz — Kahoot-style, €0/month to host, zero cold starts.
11. 
12. ## How it works
13. 
14. A **host** creates a game session with one or more wines. Each wine gets five questions (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). Participants join via a 4-digit code on their phone, answer live as the host controls the pace, and compete on a leaderboard.
15. 
16. Everything runs on **Cloudflare**: the frontend is a static Astro site on Cloudflare Pages; the game backend is a PartyKit Durable Object (Cloudflare Workers); session lists are stored in Cloudflare KV.
17. 
18. ## Quick links
19. 
20. | Doc | What it covers |
21. |-----|----------------|
22. | [Quick Start](quick-start) | Run locally in under 2 minutes |
23. | [Architecture](architecture) | Repo layout, runtime communication, DO lifecycle |
24. | [Tech Stack](tech-stack) | Stack choices and design principles |
25. | [Gameplay Workflow](gameplay-workflow) | Phase machine, event flow, answer/scoring rules |
26. | [Event Reference](event-reference) | All WebSocket message types (client ↔ server) |
27. | [Host Identity](host-identity) | `TANNIC-FALCON`-style IDs, session dashboard, rejoin |
28. | [Data Persistence](data-persistence) | DO storage keys, KV schema, what survives a restart |
29. | [Deployment](deployment) | Cloudflare Pages + PartyKit deploy guide |
30. | [Cloudflare Setup](cloudflare-setup) | Step-by-step Cloudflare dashboard walkthrough |
31. | [Contributing & Env](env) | Dev workflow, branch strategy, PR checklist, and environment setup |
32. 
33. ## What's new in v2.0 (PartyKit)
34. 
35. - **Zero cost, zero cold starts** — Durable Objects wake on demand
36. - **Session persistence** — create Monday, resume Wednesday
37. - **Participant rejoin** — page refresh during a game reconnects automatically
38. - **Host identity dashboard** — see all your past and active sessions from any device
39. - **Five questions per wine** — added `wine_name` category
40. - **Configurable timer** — 15–120 s slider at session creation
41. - **No-lock answers** — participants can change their answer until the host reveals
42. - **2 × 2 option grid** — cleaner layout on mobile
43. 
44. - [Quick Start](quick-start) — local dev setup
45. 
46.

 id: intro
 title: Sommelier Arena Docs
 sidebar_label: Introduction
 slug: /
 ---
 
 # Sommelier Arena
 
 A real-time blind wine tasting quiz — Kahoot-style, €0/month to host, zero cold starts.
 
 ## How it works
 
 A **host** creates a game session with one or more wines. Each wine gets five questions (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). Participants join via a 4-digit code on their phone, answer live as the host controls the pace, and compete on a leaderboard.
 
 Everything runs on **Cloudflare**: the frontend is a static Astro site on Cloudflare Pages; the game backend is a PartyKit Durable Object (Cloudflare Workers); session lists are stored in Cloudflare KV.
 
 ## Quick links
 
 | Doc | What it covers |
 |-----|----------------|
 | [Quick Start](quick-start) | Run locally in under 2 minutes |
 | [Architecture](architecture) | Repo layout, runtime communication, DO lifecycle |
 | [Tech Stack](tech-stack) | Stack choices and design principles |
 | [Gameplay Workflow](gameplay-workflow) | Phase machine, event flow, answer/scoring rules |
 | [Event Reference](event-reference) | All WebSocket message types (client ↔ server) |
 | [Host Identity](host-identity) | `TANNIC-FALCON`-style IDs, session dashboard, rejoin |
 | [Data Persistence](data-persistence) | DO storage keys, KV schema, what survives a restart |
 | [Deployment](deployment) | Cloudflare Pages + PartyKit deploy guide |
 | [Cloudflare Setup](cloudflare-setup) | Step-by-step Cloudflare dashboard walkthrough |
 | [Contributing & Env](env) | Dev workflow, branch strategy, PR checklist, and environment setup |
 
 ## What's new in v2.0 (PartyKit)
 
 - **Zero cost, zero cold starts** — Durable Objects wake on demand
 - **Session persistence** — create Monday, resume Wednesday
 - **Participant rejoin** — page refresh during a game reconnects automatically
 - **Host identity dashboard** — see all your past and active sessions from any device
 - **Five questions per wine** — added `wine_name` category
 - **Configurable timer** — 15–120 s slider at session creation
 - **No-lock answers** — participants can change their answer until the host reveals
 - **2 × 2 option grid** — cleaner layout on mobile
 
 - [Quick Start](quick-start) — local dev setup

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. --- 2. sidebar_position: 9 3. --- 4. 5. # Local Dev vs Production 6. 7. Understanding how the app behaves differently across environments is key to productive development and confident deployment. 8. 9. ## Services at a glance 10. 11. | | Mode A — Local dev | Mode B — Docker | Production | 12. |-|--------------------|-----------------|-----------| 13. | **Frontend** | Astro dev server
`http://localhost:4321` | nginx
`http://localhost:4321` | Cloudflare Pages (CDN) | 14. | **PartyKit backend** | `npx partykit dev`
`ws://localhost:1999` | PartyKit container
(internal, nginx proxied) | Cloudflare Workers +
Durable Objects | 15. | **Documentation** | Docusaurus dev
`http://localhost:3002` | Docusaurus container
`http://localhost:3002` | Cloudflare Pages
(separate project) | 16. | **DO storage** (`room.storage`) | ⚠️ In-memory | ⚠️ In-memory | ✅ SQLite per DO | 17. | **Cloudflare KV** (`HOSTS_KV`) | ❌ Not available | ❌ Not available | ✅ Real KV namespace | 18. | **Browser localStorage** | ✅ Works | ✅ Works | ✅ Works | 19. 20. --- 21. 22. ## How `npx partykit dev` works — no Cloudflare needed 23. 24. When you run `npm run dev` in `back/` (which runs `npx partykit dev --port 1999`), PartyKit starts a **local HTTP + WebSocket server** that emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine. 25. 26. - Each game session (`room.id` = 4-digit code) maps to an in-memory "room" on the local process. 27. - The full `Party.Room` API works: `room.storage.put/get`, `room.broadcast`, `room.storage.setAlarm`, `room.connections`. Your game code (`back/game.ts`) is **identical** in both environments — no environment-specific branches in the app code. 28. - **No Cloudflare account, no API tokens, no internet connection** is required for local development. 29. 30. **The key difference: storage is in-memory.** 31. 32. In production, `room.storage` is backed by SQLite. When a DO is evicted from memory, Cloudflare persists its storage to SQLite and restores it when the DO wakes. The `onStart()` lifecycle hook in `back/game.ts` reads saved state on wake. 33. 34. In local dev, `room.storage.put(...)` still works — data survives within a session — but restarting `partykit dev` clears everything. 35. 36. --- 37. 38. ## Storage layers demystified 39. 40. There are three distinct storage layers. Understanding which one does what — and which are unavailable locally — prevents confusion during development. 41. 42. ### 1. Browser `localStorage` — always works 43. 44. The frontend stores two things in the browser's localStorage: 45. 46. | localStorage key | Contents | Who uses it | 47. |------------------|----------|-------------| 48. | `sommelier-arena-host-{hostId}` | `SessionListEntry[]` — the host's session list | Host dashboard | 49. | `sommelier-arena-rejoin` | `{ rejoinToken, code, pseudonym }` — participant credentials | Participant rejoin | 50. 51. Managed by `front/src/lib/sessionStorage.ts`. 52. 53. **Works identically in local dev and production.** It's just the browser's own storage. 54. 55. > ⚠️ **Local dev note**: Because Cloudflare KV is not available locally (see below), the Host Dashboard session list is populated **exclusively from localStorage** in local dev. This means session history lives in your browser only — it's not shared across devices or browsers. Use the 🗑 delete button to clean up stale sessions. 56. 57. ### 2. Durable Object storage — `room.storage` — in-memory locally, SQLite in production 58. 59. The PartyKit backend persists game state per session using `room.storage`: 60. 61. | Storage key | Contents | Purpose | 62. |-------------|----------|---------| 63. | `'state'` | Full `SavedState` snapshot | Restore game state after DO eviction | 64. | `'hostId'` | String | Host re-authentication after DO restart | 65. | `'participant:{rejoinToken}'` | `Participant` data | Participant rejoin credentials | 66. | `'response:{participantId}:{questionId}'` | Answer record | Accurate scoring if DO restarts mid-game | 67. 68. **Local dev**: In-memory. Data survives while `partykit dev` is running. A process restart (Ctrl+C + re-run) loses all active sessions. 69. 70. **Production**: SQLite-backed. Data survives DO eviction. `onStart()` is called when the DO wakes from cold storage and reads back the saved state. 71. 72. ### 3. Cloudflare KV — `HOSTS_KV` — not available locally 73. 74. The backend also writes to a Cloudflare KV namespace (`HOSTS_KV`) to maintain a cross-session index per host. 75. 76. **KV key structure:** 77. ``` 78. host:{hostId} → SessionListEntry[] (list of all sessions for that host) 79. ``` 80. 81. This is used in `upsertKvSession()` in `back/game.ts` and is called when a session is created, when the game ends, etc. 82. 83. **Local dev**: The `HOSTS_KV` binding is not defined in `partykit.json`, so `room.context.bindings.HOSTS_KV` is `undefined`. The `upsertKvSession()` function is wrapped in a `try/catch` that silently skips the KV write. **This is intentional and safe** — the app continues working via localStorage. 84. 85. **Production**: A real Cloudflare KV namespace (see [Cloudflare Setup](./cloudflare-setup.md) for how to create and bind it). Enables a host to see their sessions when opening the app on a new browser or device, since the server can serve the session list from KV independent of localStorage. 86. 87. --- 88. 89. ## How a static Astro site runs multiplayer 90. 91. This might seem paradoxical: the frontend is a **static site** (no backend, no server-side rendering at request time), yet it powers live multiplayer. 92. 93. Here's how it works: 94. 95. 1. **Astro builds to pure static files** — HTML, CSS, and JavaScript bundles. No Node.js server runs at request time. Cloudflare Pages serves these files from its CDN globally. 96. 97. 2. **PartySocket is bundled in the JS** — `PartySocket` (the WebSocket client library for PartyKit) is imported in the frontend code and compiled into the static JS bundle. 98. 99. 3. **On page load, the browser establishes a WebSocket connection** to the PartyKit backend: 100. ``` 101. ws://your-domain.com/parties/main/{sessionCode} 102. ``` 103. (In local dev: `ws://localhost:1999/parties/main/{sessionCode}`) 104. 105. 4. **All game state flows over WebSocket events** — there is no REST API. When the host starts the game, sends a `host:start` message. When a participant answers, sends `participant:submit_answer`. The backend broadcasts `game:question`, `game:timer_tick`, `game:answer_revealed`, etc. 106. 107. 5. **React islands handle reactivity** — Astro pages are static shells; the interactive game UI is rendered by React components (hydrated client-side with `client:only="react"`). Zustand stores (`hostStore`, `participantStore`) hold all game state client-side and are updated by socket hook event handlers. 108. 109. ``` 110. Browser Cloudflare (production) 111. │ │ 112. │ GET https://your-domain.com/host │ 113. │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Pages CDN 114. │ <─────────────────────────────────────│ returns static HTML + JS 115. │ │ 116. │ WS wss://your-domain.com/parties/main/1234 117. │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Workers 118. │ <═════════════════════════════════════│ Durable Object (game.ts) 119. │ real-time game events (JSON) │ 120. ``` 121. 122. **The static frontend is a WebSocket client application packaged as static files.** This pattern achieves global distribution (CDN) with real-time interactivity (WebSocket) at very low cost. 123. 124. --- 125. 126. ## Quick reference: what to check when things behave differently 127. 128. | Symptom | Likely cause | 129. |---------|-------------| 130. | Session history missing after restart | Expected in dev — DO storage + KV are ephemeral locally | 131. | Host dashboard shows sessions from previous dev session | localStorage persists across restarts; use 🗑 to clean up | 132. | Participant can't rejoin after `partykit dev` restart | Expected — rejoin tokens are stored in DO storage (in-memory) | 133. | Session list only shows in one browser | KV is not available locally; localStorage is per-browser | 134. | Works locally but missing sessions in prod | `HOSTS_KV` binding not configured — see [Cloudflare Setup](./cloudflare-setup.md) | 135.

Zustand stores (`hostStore`, `participantStore`) hold all game state client-side and are updated by socket hook event handlers. ``` Browser Cloudflare (production) │ │ │ GET https://your-domain.com/host │ │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Pages CDN │ <─────────────────────────────────────│ returns static HTML + JS │ │ │ WS wss://your-domain.com/parties/main/1234 │ ─────────────────────────────────────>│ Cloudflare Workers │ <═════════════════════════════════════│ Durable Object (game.ts) │ real-time game events (JSON) │ ``` **The static frontend is a WebSocket client application packaged as static files.** This pattern achieves global distribution (CDN) with real-time interactivity (WebSocket) at very low cost. --- ## Quick reference: what to check when things behave differently | Symptom | Likely cause | |---------|-------------| | Session history missing after restart | Expected in dev — DO storage + KV are ephemeral locally | | Host dashboard shows sessions from previous dev session | localStorage persists across restarts; use 🗑 to clean up | | Participant can't rejoin after `partykit dev` restart | Expected — rejoin tokens are stored in DO storage (in-memory) | | Session list only shows in one browser | KV is not available locally; localStorage is per-browser | | Works locally but missing sessions in prod | `HOSTS_KV` binding not configured — see [Cloudflare Setup](./cloudflare-setup.md) |

arguments.file_text:
name: Docs CI

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'docs-site/**'
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'docs-site/**'

jobs:
  build-docs:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - name: Install deps
        run: |
          cd docs-site
          npm ci
      - name: Build docs
        run: |
          cd docs-site
          npm run build
      - name: Check for broken links
        run: |
          # Docusaurus build already warns about broken links; fail if any 'WARN' appears
          cd docs-site
          npm run build 2>&1 | tee build.log || true
          if grep -n "\[WARN\|warning\]" build.log; then
            echo "Warnings found in docs build:"; grep -n "\[WARN\|warning\]" build.log; exit 1
          fi

./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:Note about PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST and CI
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- In this repository `app-ci.yml` runs `npx partykit deploy` as part of the CI deploy job. That command produces the PartyKit project URL (the value you must set for `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST`). The simplest ways to ensure the Pages project receives the correct value are:
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:  1) Manual one-time: run `npx partykit deploy` locally, copy the printed URL, and set `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` in the Pages project's Environment variables. Then trigger a redeploy.
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:echo "PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST"
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:To apply `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` to Cloudflare Pages automatically you can either:
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Build the frontend in CI using the captured `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` and then publish the built static site to Pages. This approach avoids mutating Pages project environment variables: the CI build bakes the correct host into the static assets and then runs `npx wrangler pages publish` to deploy them. Required secrets: `CF_API_TOKEN` and `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME`.
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:      echo "PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$HOSTNAME" >> $GITHUB_ENV
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:    PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Or, if you prefer to update the Pages project environment variable (`PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST`) directly via the Pages API, add the `CF_ACCOUNT_ID` and `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME` secrets and use the Pages REST API to upsert the variable. This requires additional Pages API permissions for the `CF_API_TOKEN`. The build+publish approach is simpler and idempotent.
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:  To apply `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` to Cloudflare Pages automatically you can:
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:  - Use the Cloudflare Pages REST API to set environment variables for the project (requires CF_API_TOKEN and the account + project identifiers). Add a CI step that calls the Pages API to update or create the `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` variable and then trigger a Pages redeploy; or
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:  - Build the frontend within the same CI job after capturing `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` by invoking the front build using the env var in-process (e.g. `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build`) and then deploy that built artifact to Pages via the Pages Deployments API (more complex).
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:  2. Calls the Pages API to upsert `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` using `CF_API_TOKEN` + `CF_ACCOUNT_ID` + `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME` (these need adding as GitHub Secrets)
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:If you need to update `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` after initial deploy:
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- How to obtain `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` (PartyKit project URL):
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- Once you have the URL, set `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` in Pages settings to the hostname only (no trailing slash), e.g. `sommelier-arena.<your-account>.partykit.dev`. Then trigger a redeploy to apply the env var.
./docs-site/docs/cloudflare-setup.md:- [ ] `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` env var set in Pages to your partykit.dev URL
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=localhost:1999
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=localhost:4321   ← set via Docker build arg
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:- The Astro build is **static** — `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` is baked at build time, not runtime. There is no way to change it after the image is built.
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:REDACTED.USERNAME.partykit.dev
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:| Mode | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Who proxies? |
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:| `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | `front/.env.local` (Mode A) | `localhost:1999` |
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:| `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | `docker-compose.yml` build arg (Mode B) | `localhost:4321` |
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:| `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard (Production) | `<project>.partykit.dev` |
./docs-site/docs/networking.md:> **localhost:1999 vs localhost:4321 in dev:** When running Mode A (local dev with `npx partykit dev`), PartyKit listens on port **1999** directly. Your browser connects to `ws://localhost:1999/parties/main/{code}`. In Mode B (Docker), PartyKit is internal-only; nginx at port **4321** accepts WebSocket upgrades at `/parties/*` and proxies them to the PartyKit container. The app code is identical in both modes — only `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` changes.

arguments.file_text:
---
id: config-comparison
title: Configuration Comparison — Local vs Production
sidebar_label: Config Comparison
audience: developer
tags: [config, deployment, env]
---

# Configuration Comparison — Local vs Production

This table shows where key configuration values live and how they differ between local development modes and production (Cloudflare) deployments.

| Layer | Setting | Local (Mode A) | Docker (Mode B) | Production (Cloudflare) |
|-------|---------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Frontend | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | front/.env.local (`localhost:1999`) | Docker build arg / env `localhost:4321` | Cloudflare Pages env or baked at build (e.g. `sommelier-arena.<username>.partykit.dev`) |
| Docs | Docs base URL | docs-site dev (`http://localhost:3002`) | docs container (`http://localhost:3002`) | Cloudflare Pages (`https://<project>.pages.dev` or custom domain `/docs`) |
| Backend | PartyKit host | `npx partykit dev` (localhost:1999) | PartyKit container (internal) | Cloudflare Workers (Durable Objects) |
| KV | `HOSTS_KV` binding | Not available (local) | Not available (docker-mode without binding) | Cloudflare KV namespace bound via partykit.json or Wrangler |
| Proxy Worker | DOCS_ORIGIN | N/A | N/A | `DOCS_ORIGIN` injected via Wrangler `--var` or Worker env var (points to Pages project) |
| Docker | Compose file | N/A | `docker-compose.yml` (services: nginx, back, front, docs) | N/A |
| Deployment | Deploy command | `npx partykit deploy` for PartyKit local/test | `docker-compose up --build` for integration testing | Git push / Pages auto-deploy; `npx partykit deploy` for PartyKit; `npx wrangler deploy` for Worker |

## Notes
- `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` is baked into the frontend at build time. For Cloudflare Pages, choose either to set it as a Pages Environment Variable or bake it into the artifact during CI and deploy the artifact to Pages.
- `HOSTS_KV` must be created in the Cloudflare account and the namespace id added to `partykit.json` (or managed via Wrangler KV namespace bindings).
- The proxy worker's `DOCS_ORIGIN` should be provided by CI after docs Pages deploys and returns its pages.dev URL.

<!-- End of config comparison -->

arguments.file_text:
---
id: cloudflare-wrangler
title: Cloudflare & Wrangler — CLI-first deploy
sidebar_label: Wrangler & Cloudflare
audience: developer
tags: [cloudflare, wrangler, ci, deploy]
---

# Cloudflare & Wrangler — CLI-first deploy

This guide shows reproducible, scriptable commands to manage Cloudflare resources for Sommelier Arena using Wrangler and the Cloudflare API. Prefer CLI automation over Dashboard steps for CI and reproducibility.

## Prerequisites

- `npx wrangler` available (no global install required)
- `CF_API_TOKEN` with permissions for Pages, Workers, KV, and Routes (store in GitHub Actions secrets)
- `CF_ACCOUNT_ID` and `ZONE_ID` for your Cloudflare account
- `PARTYKIT` credentials: ability to run `npx partykit deploy` (CI must have access)

## Create a KV namespace with Wrangler

```bash
# Create KV namespace and capture id (requires CF_ACCOUNT_ID env)
npx wrangler kv:namespace create "SOMMELIER_HOSTS" --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID
# Wrangler prints: Created namespace with id: xxxxxxxx
```

Add the namespace id to `partykit.json` (or manage it via wrangler bindings):

```json
{ "binding": "HOSTS_KV", "id": "YOUR_KV_NAMESPACE_ID" }
```

Alternatively, declare the KV namespace in `wrangler.toml` and use `kv_namespaces` bindings.

## Deploy PartyKit in CI and capture the URL

In CI (or locally) run PartyKit deploy and extract the published host. The PartyKit deploy step prints the URL (e.g., `https://sommelier-arena.YOURNAME.partykit.dev`). Use that as `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` when building the frontend.

Example (bash):

```bash
# Run PartyKit deploy and capture the URL
PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
# Extract hostname (simple grep; adjust if PartyKit output changes)
PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev")
PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST"
```

## Build frontend with the baked-in host

The Astro frontend bakes `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` at build time. In CI, set it when building.

```bash
# Build front with the correct host baked into the static assets
PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
```

## Publish frontend to Cloudflare Pages (two options)

Option A — wrangler pages publish (publish built static files):

```bash
# Publish built frontend directory to Pages (requires project name configured in wrangler.toml)
npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME --branch main
```

Option B — Use Pages REST API to create a deployment (more steps but avoids needing wrangler pages): build and then call the Pages API to upload the asset manifest and static files. See Cloudflare Pages API docs.

## Deploy the Proxy Worker with environment variables

Use Wrangler to compile and publish the TypeScript worker and inject runtime variables like `DOCS_ORIGIN`:

```bash
# Deploy proxy worker and set DOCS_ORIGIN so the worker knows where to fetch docs
npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://$CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME.pages.dev
```

If you need to create a route programmatically (e.g., `/docs*`), use the Cloudflare REST API:

```bash
curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $CF_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{ "pattern": "sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs*", "script": "sommelier-arena-proxy" }'
```

## Recommended GitHub Actions CI snippet

Below is a compact example workflow that:
1. Runs `npx partykit deploy` to publish PartyKit and capture the host
2. Builds the frontend with the captured host
3. Publishes the frontend to Pages using `wrangler pages publish`
4. Deploys the proxy worker with `wrangler deploy`

Save as `.github/workflows/deploy-example.yml` (example):

```yaml
name: Deploy to Cloudflare (example)
on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      CF_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CF_API_TOKEN }}
      CF_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCOUNT_ID }}
      CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME: ${{ secrets.CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME }}
      ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.ZONE_ID }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - name: Install deps
        run: |
          npm ci
      - name: Deploy PartyKit (publish DOs) and capture host
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
          echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT"
          PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev" || true)
          if [ -z "$PARTYKIT_URL" ]; then echo "PartyKit deploy did not return a URL"; exit 1; fi
          PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
          echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      - name: Build frontend
        run: |
          PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
      - name: Publish frontend to Pages
        run: |
          npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME
      - name: Deploy proxy worker
        run: |
          npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://${CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME}.pages.dev
```

Notes:
- Store `CF_API_TOKEN`, `CF_ACCOUNT_ID`, `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME`, and `ZONE_ID` in GitHub Secrets.
- The PartyKit deploy step requires credentials. If PartyKit cannot run in CI (e.g., needs interactive login), consider running PartyKit deploy outside CI and set `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` via Pages Environment Variables instead.

## Automating Routes and Environment Variables via API (optional)

If you prefer to avoid `wrangler deploy --var`, use the Pages and Workers REST APIs to programmatically upsert environment variables and create routes. This requires a `CF_API_TOKEN` with appropriate scopes. See Cloudflare API docs for `Pages` and `Workers` endpoints.

## Troubleshooting

- If `wrangler deploy` fails with permission errors, ensure the `CF_API_TOKEN` has `Workers:Edit` and `Account:Read` scopes.
- If Pages publish fails, verify `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME` and that the token has Pages permissions.
- When testing locally, run `npx wrangler whoami` to confirm authentication.

<!-- End Cloudflare Wrangler guidance -->

id: cloudflare-wrangler
title: Cloudflare & Wrangler — CLI-first deploy
sidebar_label: Wrangler & Cloudflare
audience: developer
tags: [cloudflare, wrangler, ci, deploy]
---

# Cloudflare & Wrangler — CLI-first deploy

This guide shows reproducible, scriptable commands to manage Cloudflare resources for Sommelier Arena using Wrangler and the Cloudflare API. Prefer CLI automation over Dashboard steps for CI and reproducibility.

## Prerequisites

- `npx wrangler` available (no global install required)
- `CF_API_TOKEN` with permissions for Pages, Workers, KV, and Routes (store in GitHub Actions secrets)
- `CF_ACCOUNT_ID` and `ZONE_ID` for your Cloudflare account
- `PARTYKIT` credentials: ability to run `npx partykit deploy` (CI must have access)

## Create a KV namespace with Wrangler

```bash
# Create KV namespace and capture id (requires CF_ACCOUNT_ID env)
npx wrangler kv:namespace create "SOMMELIER_HOSTS" --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID
# Wrangler prints: Created namespace with id: xxxxxxxx
```

Add the namespace id to `partykit.json` (or manage it via wrangler bindings):

```json
{ "binding": "HOSTS_KV", "id": "YOUR_KV_NAMESPACE_ID" }
```

Alternatively, declare the KV namespace in `wrangler.toml` and use `kv_namespaces` bindings.

## Deploy PartyKit in CI and capture the URL

In CI (or locally) run PartyKit deploy and extract the published host. The PartyKit deploy step prints the URL (e.g., `https://sommelier-arena.YOURNAME.partykit.dev`). Use that as `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` when building the frontend.

Example (bash):

```bash
# Run PartyKit deploy and capture the URL
PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
# Extract hostname (simple grep; adjust if PartyKit output changes)
PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev")
PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST"
```

## Build frontend with the baked-in host

The Astro frontend bakes `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` at build time. In CI, set it when building.

```bash
# Build front with the correct host baked into the static assets
PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
```

## Publish frontend to Cloudflare Pages (two options)

Option A — wrangler pages publish (publish built static files):

```bash
# Publish built frontend directory to Pages (requires project name configured in wrangler.toml)
npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME --branch main
```

Option B — Use Pages REST API to create a deployment (more steps but avoids needing wrangler pages): build and then call the Pages API to upload the asset manifest and static files. See Cloudflare Pages API docs.

## Deploy the Proxy Worker with environment variables

Use Wrangler to compile and publish the TypeScript worker and inject runtime variables like `DOCS_ORIGIN`:

```bash
# Deploy proxy worker and set DOCS_ORIGIN so the worker knows where to fetch docs
npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://$CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME.pages.dev
```

If you need to create a route programmatically (e.g., `/docs*`), use the Cloudflare REST API:

```bash
curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $CF_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{ "pattern": "sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs*", "script": "sommelier-arena-proxy" }'
```

## Recommended GitHub Actions CI snippet

Below is a compact example workflow that:
1. Runs `npx partykit deploy` to publish PartyKit and capture the host
2. Builds the frontend with the captured host
3. Publishes the frontend to Pages using `wrangler pages publish`
4. Deploys the proxy worker with `wrangler deploy`

Save as `.github/workflows/deploy-example.yml` (example):

```yaml
name: Deploy to Cloudflare (example)
on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      CF_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CF_API_TOKEN }}
      CF_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCOUNT_ID }}
      CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME: ${{ secrets.CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME }}
      ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.ZONE_ID }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - name: Install deps
        run: |
          npm ci
      - name: Deploy PartyKit (publish DOs) and capture host
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
          echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT"
          PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev" || true)
          if [ -z "$PARTYKIT_URL" ]; then echo "PartyKit deploy did not return a URL"; exit 1; fi
          PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
          echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      - name: Build frontend
        run: |
          PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
      - name: Publish frontend to Pages
        run: |
          npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME
      - name: Deploy proxy worker
        run: |
          npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://${CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME}.pages.dev
```

Notes:
- Store `CF_API_TOKEN`, `CF_ACCOUNT_ID`, `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME`, and `ZONE_ID` in GitHub Secrets.
- The PartyKit deploy step requires credentials. If PartyKit cannot run in CI (e.g., needs interactive login), consider running PartyKit deploy outside CI and set `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` via Pages Environment Variables instead.

## Automating Routes and Environment Variables via API (optional)

If you prefer to avoid `wrangler deploy --var`, use the Pages and Workers REST APIs to programmatically upsert environment variables and create routes. This requires a `CF_API_TOKEN` with appropriate scopes. See Cloudflare API docs for `Pages` and `Workers` endpoints.

## Troubleshooting

- If `wrangler deploy` fails with permission errors, ensure the `CF_API_TOKEN` has `Workers:Edit` and `Account:Read` scopes.
- If Pages publish fails, verify `CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME` and that the token has Pages permissions.
- When testing locally, run `npx wrangler whoami` to confirm authentication.

<!-- End Cloudflare Wrangler guidance -->

      - name: Deploy proxy worker

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      CF_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CF_API_TOKEN }}
      CF_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCOUNT_ID }}
      CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME: ${{ secrets.CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME }}
      ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.ZONE_ID }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - name: Install deps
        run: npm ci
      - name: Deploy PartyKit and capture host
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
          echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT"
          PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev" || true)
          if [ -z "$PARTYKIT_URL" ]; then echo "PartyKit deploy did not return a URL"; exit 1; fi
          PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
          echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      - name: Build and publish frontend
        run: |
          PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
          npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME
      - name: Deploy proxy worker
        run: |
          npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://${CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME}.pages.dev
"}

toolArgs.file_text:
name: Deploy Example (Cloudflare)

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      CF_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CF_API_TOKEN }}
      CF_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCOUNT_ID }}
      CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME: ${{ secrets.CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME }}
      ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.ZONE_ID }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - name: Install deps
        run: npm ci
      - name: Deploy PartyKit and capture host
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          PARTYKIT_OUTPUT=$(npx partykit deploy 2>&1)
          echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT"
          PARTYKIT_URL=$(echo "$PARTYKIT_OUTPUT" | grep -oE "https?://[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.partykit\.dev" || true)
          if [ -z "$PARTYKIT_URL" ]; then echo "PartyKit deploy did not return a URL"; exit 1; fi
          PARTYKIT_HOST=${PARTYKIT_URL#https://}
          echo "PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      - name: Build and publish frontend
        run: |
          PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=$PARTYKIT_HOST npm --prefix front run build
          npx wrangler pages publish ./front/dist --project-name $CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME
      - name: Deploy proxy worker
        run: |
          npx wrangler deploy --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://${CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME}.pages.dev

data.content:
Done: added CLI-first Cloudflare guidance and CI examples.

What I added
- docs-site/docs/cloudflare-wrangler.md — comprehensive Wrangler + Cloudflare CLI guide (KV, PartyKit in CI, Pages publish, Wrangler deploy, route creation via API), with GitHub Actions example.
- .github/workflows/deploy-example.yml — concrete example workflow (workflow_dispatch) illustrating PartyKit deploy, building front with PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST, publishing Pages, and deploying proxy worker.
- Marked the cloudflare-scripts todo as done.

Notes & safety
- The example CI requires secrets: CF_API_TOKEN, CF_ACCOUNT_ID, CF_PAGES_PROJECT_NAME, ZONE_ID. It also requires PartyKit credentials; if PartyKit cannot run non-interactively in your CI, set PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST via Pages environment variables instead (documented in the guide).
- The workflows are examples and are not triggered automatically except via manual dispatch.

Next recommended steps (I can continue autonomously)
1. docs-automation: finalize CI checks (extend docs CI to fail on broken links and generate the machine-readable index from per-file frontmatter).  
2. cleanup-core: sweep remaining docs to remove or merge outdated content and ensure all pages have frontmatter tags (audience, tags, summary).  
3. readme-and-prd: generate README.md and PRD link-based file using the SKILL templates.

Which should I do next? If no preference, proceed to implement docs-automation (add link-checker and frontmatter index generator) and then run it locally to produce an updated docs-index.json.

arguments.file_text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Simple index generator for docs frontmatter (no external deps)
set -euo pipefail
out=docs-site/docs/docs-index.json
echo '{"generated_at":"'"$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"'","pages":['> "$out"
first=true
for f in docs-site/docs/*.md; do
  path=$(basename "$f")
  title=$(grep -m1 '^title:' "$f" | sed 's/^title:[[:space:]]*//; s/"//g' || true)
  if [ -z "$title" ]; then title=$(grep -m1 '^# ' "$f" | sed 's/^# //' || true); fi
  audience=$(grep -m1 '^audience:' "$f" | sed 's/^audience:[[:space:]]*//; s/"//g' || true)
  tags=$(grep -m1 '^tags:' "$f" | sed 's/^tags:[[:space:]]*//; s/\[//; s/\]//; s/"//g' || true)
  if [ "$first" = true ]; then first=false; else echo ',' >> "$out"; fi
  echo -n "  { \"path\": \"$path\", \"title\": \"$title\", \"audience\": \"$audience\", \"tags\": [\"$(echo "$tags" | sed 's/,/\", \"/g')\"] }" >> "$out"

done

echo '] }' >> "$out"

echo "Wrote $out"

+  if [ -z "$title" ]; then title=$(grep -m1 '^# ' "$f" | sed 's/^# //' || true); fi
+  audience=$(grep -m1 '^audience:' "$f" | sed 's/^audience:[[:space:]]*//; s/"//g' || true)
+  tags=$(grep -m1 '^tags:' "$f" | sed 's/^tags:[[:space:]]*//; s/\[//; s/\]//; s/"//g' || true)
+  if [ "$first" = true ]; then first=false; else echo ',' >> "$out"; fi
+  echo -n "  { \"path\": \"$path\", \"title\": \"$title\", \"audience\": \"$audience\", \"tags\": [\"$(echo "$tags" | sed 's/,/\", \"/g')\"] }" >> "$out"
+
+done

+echo '] }' >> "$out"

diff --git a/Users/mac-FDUCAT18/Workspace/FDUCAT/SommelierArena/docs-site/scripts/generate-index.sh b/Users/mac-FDUCAT18/Workspace/FDUCAT/SommelierArena/docs-site/scripts/generate-index.sh
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/Users/mac-FDUCAT18/Workspace/FDUCAT/SommelierArena/docs-site/scripts/generate-index.sh
@@ -1,0 +1,21 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+# Simple index generator for docs frontmatter (no external deps)
+set -euo pipefail
+out=docs-site/docs/docs-index.json
+echo '{"generated_at":"'"$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"'","pages":['> "$out"
+first=true
+for f in docs-site/docs/*.md; do
+  path=$(basename "$f")
+  title=$(grep -m1 '^title:' "$f" | sed 's/^title:[[:space:]]*//; s/"//g' || true)
+  if [ -z "$title" ]; then title=$(grep -m1 '^# ' "$f" | sed 's/^# //' || true); fi
+  audience=$(grep -m1 '^audience:' "$f" | sed 's/^audience:[[:space:]]*//; s/"//g' || true)
+  tags=$(grep -m1 '^tags:' "$f" | sed 's/^tags:[[:space:]]*//; s/\[//; s/\]//; s/"//g' || true)
+  if [ "$first" = true ]; then first=false; else echo ',' >> "$out"; fi
+  echo -n "  { \"path\": \"$path\", \"title\": \"$title\", \"audience\": \"$audience\", \"tags\": [\"$(echo "$tags" | sed 's/,/\", \"/g')\"] }" >> "$out"
+
+done
+
+echo '] }' >> "$out"
+
+echo "Wrote $out"
+

Docs automation implemented:

- Added simple generator script docs-site/scripts/generate-index.sh that scans docs frontmatter and writes docs-site/docs/docs-index.json.
- Ran the script and created an updated docs-index.json.
- Added a GitHub Actions docs CI earlier; now the index generator can be added to CI if desired.
- Marked docs-automation todo as done.

Remaining major tasks:
- cleanup-core (content sweep, polish, remove any remaining outdated instructions)
- readme-and-prd (generate README.md and PRD link-based)
- review-qa (manual review, finalize docs)

Next recommendation: run cleanup-core to finish merging/rewriting remaining outdated content and add frontmatter tags where missing. Proceed to that step now? (I'll continue unless you instruct otherwise.)

1. Environment variables and .env strategy (full-stack)
2. 
3. This document explains how environment variables are managed across the monorepo (front, back, docs-site) and how to configure them for local development, Docker, and production.
4. 
5. Principles and precedence
6. 
7. - Per-service `.env` files are the primary source for local development. Each service (front/, back/, docs-site/) should include a `.env.example` describing required variables.
8. - Runtime environment (CI, Docker build args, platform env) overrides per-service `.env` values and is authoritative in production.
9. - Root `.env` is discouraged and not used; keep per-service examples and copy them to `.env` when developing locally.
10. - Never commit real secrets. Use CI/Platform secret stores for production values.
11. 
12. Per-service notes
13. 
14. - Frontend (front/)
15.   - Template: `front/.env.local.example` → copy to `front/.env.local` for local dev.
16.   - Key var: PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST (Mode A: localhost:1999; Mode B/Docker: localhost:4321 baked at build time)
17.   - In Docker mode the build injects the correct host; do not rely on local `.env.local` for Docker builds.
18. 
19. - Backend (back/)
20.   - Template: `back/.env.example` documents backend-only envs.
21.   - Durable Object bindings (KV, etc.) are configured in `wrangler.toml` and secrets are created via `wrangler secret put` or set in CI. Do not rely on `.env` for production DO bindings.
22. 
23. - Docs (docs-site/)
24.   - Template: `docs-site/.env.example` → copy to `docs-site/.env` to mirror production (`DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs`) or set to `/` for root serving.
25.   - The Dockerfile accepts `--build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL` to bake the base URL at build time.
26. 
27. How to use locally
28. 
29. 1. Copy the appropriate example into a `.env` (or `.env.local` for the front) in the service folder:
30. 
31.    cd front && cp .env.local.example .env.local
32.    cd back  && cp .env.example .env
33.    cd docs-site && cp .env.example .env
34. 
35. 2. Install dependencies and run the service (example for docs-site):
36. 
37.    cd docs-site
38.    npm ci
39.    npm run build:local   # uses DOCS_BASE_URL from .env
40.    npm run serve:build
41. 
42. Docker and CI
43. 
44. - Docker builds should pass necessary build args or envs explicitly; do not bake secrets into images.
45.   Example: `docker build --build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs -t sommelier-docs .`
46. - CI should set required environment variables via secrets and pass them into build jobs. Runtime envs in CI override `.env` files.
47. 
48. Troubleshooting
49. 
50. - If a local build yields incorrect base paths, ensure `DOCS_BASE_URL` is set in the service's `.env` or passed via the build command.
51. - To verify which vars are picked up, run a local script that prints key env vars (do not print secrets to logs).
52. 
53. Where to find examples
54. 
55. - front/.env.local.example
56. - back/.env.example
57. - docs-site/.env.example
58. 
59. Change log
60. 
61. - Consolidated environment guidance into this file; removed redundant CONTRIBUTING.md at repository root to avoid duplication.
62. 
63. If you want this doc promoted into a top-level CONTRIBUTING.md instead, say so and it can replace the deleted file.
64.
