5. 
6. # Overview
7. 
8. Sommelier Arena is a lightweight real-time quiz app for blind wine tasting with friends. A host configures a test (a list of wines) and players join with a 4-digit code. The session is played in real-time: for each wine the host reveals one question at a time, participants answer, and the host reveals the correct answer before moving on.
9. 
10. ## Core concepts
11. 
12. - **Session**: a single game instance, identified by a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`). Ephemeral — lives in memory only.
13. - **Round**: one wine. Each round consists of exactly 5 questions played back-to-back. The leaderboard is shown after all 5 questions in a round are completed, before the next wine begins.
14. - **Question**: one of the 5 fixed-category questions for a wine (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). The host pre-fills the correct answer and 3 distractors for each.
15. 
16. ## Key constraints for MVP
17. 
18. - Max simultaneous players: 10 (strict limit; lobby rejects extra joins)
19. - No mid-session joins — once the host starts, the lobby is closed
20. - Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory / client-side state only); no server-side persistence, no export/import
21. - Hosts have no persistent accounts; the host session is tied to the browser tab that created it
22. - Exactly 5 questions per wine; categories are fixed (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name)
23. - Timer per question: 60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP); resumes from where it was paused on Resume — it does not restart
24. - Scoring: fixed 100 points per correct answer; 0 points if unanswered or wrong; host cannot change point values
25. - Answer lock: first tap locks in a participant's answer — it cannot be changed

 id: overview
 title: "Overview"
 ---
 
 # Overview
 
 Sommelier Arena is a lightweight real-time quiz app for blind wine tasting with friends. A host configures a test (a list of wines) and players join with a 4-digit code. The session is played in real-time: for each wine the host reveals one question at a time, participants answer, and the host reveals the correct answer before moving on.
 
 ## Core concepts
 
 - **Session**: a single game instance, identified by a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`). Ephemeral — lives in memory only.
 - **Round**: one wine. Each round consists of exactly 5 questions played back-to-back. The leaderboard is shown after all 5 questions in a round are completed, before the next wine begins.
 - **Question**: one of the 5 fixed-category questions for a wine (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). The host pre-fills the correct answer and 3 distractors for each.
 
 ## Key constraints for MVP
 
 - Max simultaneous players: 10 (strict limit; lobby rejects extra joins)
 - No mid-session joins — once the host starts, the lobby is closed
 - Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory / client-side state only); no server-side persistence, no export/import
 - Hosts have no persistent accounts; the host session is tied to the browser tab that created it
 - Exactly 5 questions per wine; categories are fixed (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name)
 - Timer per question: 60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP); resumes from where it was paused on Resume — it does not restart
 - Scoring: fixed 100 points per correct answer; 0 points if unanswered or wrong; host cannot change point values
 - Answer lock: first tap locks in a participant's answer — it cannot be changed

4. sidebar_label: Quick Start

8. 
9. > NOTE: Deployment and Pages instructions formerly here have moved to [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy). Quick Start still contains local development modes; see the canonical deployment doc for production.
10. 
11. <!-- Partial page preserved; refer to canonical docs for deploy steps. -->
12. 
13. 
14. # Quick Start
15. 
16. Three development modes depending on what you're testing.
17. 
18. ## Prerequisites
19. 
20. - Node.js 20+
21. - `git clone` the repo and `cd SommelierArena`
22. 
23. ## Mode A — Fast daily dev (recommended)
24. 
25. Best for feature work. No Docker needed.
26. 
27. ```bash
28. # Terminal 1 — PartyKit backend (port 1999)
29. npx partykit dev
30. ```
31. 
32. :::tip No Cloudflare account needed for local dev
33. `npx partykit dev` runs a **local in-memory simulator** — it emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine with no internet connection required. Note: Cloudflare KV (`HOSTS_KV`) is not available locally; session history comes from browser localStorage only. See [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) for the full comparison.
34. :::
35. 
36. # Terminal 2 — Astro frontend (port 4321)
37. cd front
38. cp .env.local.example .env.local   # PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=localhost:1999
39. npm run dev
40. ```
41. 
42. Open `http://localhost:4321/host` (host) and `http://localhost:4321/play` (participant).
43. 
44. ## Mode B — Full integration (Docker)
45. 
46. Best for E2E tests and nginx/proxy validation.
47. 
48. ```bash
49. docker-compose up --build
50. ```
51. 
52. | Service | URL |
53. |---------|-----|
54. | Frontend (nginx) | `http://localhost:4321` |
55. | PartyKit backend | `http://localhost:1999` |
56. | Docs (Docusaurus) | `http://localhost:3002` |
57. 
58. ### Docker cheat sheet
59. 
60. ```bash
61. # Start the full stack
62. docker-compose up --build -d
63. 
64. # Stop the full stack
65. docker-compose down
66. 
67. # Rebuild a single service
68. docker-compose up --build -d front
69. 
70. # View logs
71. docker-compose logs -f
72. ```
73. 
74. > ⚠️ **Important:** Use `docker-compose down` to stop the running stack. If you started the stack with additional flags or in a different context, ensure you stop the correct compose project instance.
75. 
76. ## Mode C — Docs only
77. 
78. ```bash
79. cd docs-site
80. npm run start:local
81. # → http://localhost:3002
82. ```
83. 
84. ### Docs site — local search & preview
85. 
86. This project uses a local, file-based search plugin for Docusaurus to provide a search box in the docs navbar.
87. 
88. Quick start (dev):
89. 
90. 1. Install dependencies:
91. 
92.    ```bash
93.    cd docs-site
94.    npm ci
95.    ```
96. 
97. 2. Start the dev server (live reload):
98. 
99.    ```bash
100.    npm run start:local
101.    ```
102. 
103. 3. Open http://localhost:3002 and use the search box in the navbar.
104. 
105. Preview built site (parity with production)
106. 
107. This repository standardizes on building the docs for deployment under `/docs` (Cloudflare Pages). To preview the site exactly as production will serve it:
108. 
109. ```bash
110. # Build with DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs
111. cd docs-site
112. npm run build:local
113. 
114. # Serve the built site mounted at /docs
115. npm run serve:docs
116. # → http://localhost:3002/docs
117. ```
118. 
119. If you prefer to preview the site at root (`/`), build with `DOCS_BASE_URL=/` and use `npm run serve` to open http://localhost:3002/.
120. 
121. Notes
122. - The plugin dependency (@cmfcmf/docusaurus-search-local) is declared in package.json and will be installed by `npm ci`.
123. - The site configuration in docusaurus.config.ts will load the plugin if installed. If you build the docs inside Docker or CI, `npm ci` in the Dockerfile will ensure the plugin is available at build time.
124. - If `npm ci` fails in your environment, inspect the npm logs and ensure a network/proxy is configured correctly.
125. - See the Testing & Preview page for more details: [Testing & Preview](./testing-and-preview).
126. 
127. ## Run tests
128. 
129. ```bash
130. # Frontend unit tests (Vitest + RTL)
131. cd front && npm test
132. 
133. # E2E tests (requires Mode B Docker stack running)
134. cd e2e && npm test -- --project=chromium
135. ```
136. 
137. ## Environment variables
138. 
139. | Variable | Where | Value |
140. |----------|-------|-------|
141. | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | `front/.env.local` | `localhost:1999` (local) |
142. | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard | `sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev` (prod) |
143. 
144. See `front/.env.local.example` for a template.
145. 
146. > **Note:** Sessions persist in your browser's localStorage. Use the 🗑 button on the Host Dashboard to clean up old sessions.
147.

 > NOTE: Deployment and Pages instructions formerly here have moved to [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy). Quick Start still contains local development modes; see the canonical deployment doc for production.
 
 <!-- Partial page preserved; refer to canonical docs for deploy steps. -->
 
 
 # Quick Start
 
 Three development modes depending on what you're testing.
 
 ## Prerequisites
 
 - Node.js 20+
 - `git clone` the repo and `cd SommelierArena`
 
 ## Mode A — Fast daily dev (recommended)
 
 Best for feature work. No Docker needed.
 
 ```bash
 # Terminal 1 — PartyKit backend (port 1999)
 npx partykit dev
 ```
 
 :::tip No Cloudflare account needed for local dev
 `npx partykit dev` runs a **local in-memory simulator** — it emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine with no internet connection required. Note: Cloudflare KV (`HOSTS_KV`) is not available locally; session history comes from browser localStorage only. See [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) for the full comparison.
 :::
 
 # Terminal 2 — Astro frontend (port 4321)
 cd front
 cp .env.local.example .env.local   # PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=localhost:1999
 npm run dev
 ```
 
 Open `http://localhost:4321/host` (host) and `http://localhost:4321/play` (participant).
 
 ## Mode B — Full integration (Docker)
 
 Best for E2E tests and nginx/proxy validation.
 
 ```bash
 docker-compose up --build
 ```
 
 | Service | URL |
 |---------|-----|
 | Frontend (nginx) | `http://localhost:4321` |
 | PartyKit backend | `http://localhost:1999` |
 | Docs (Docusaurus) | `http://localhost:3002` |
 
 ### Docker cheat sheet
 
 ```bash
 # Start the full stack
 docker-compose up --build -d
 
 # Stop the full stack
 docker-compose down
 
 # Rebuild a single service
 docker-compose up --build -d front
 
 # View logs
 docker-compose logs -f
 ```
 
 > ⚠️ **Important:** Use `docker-compose down` to stop the running stack. If you started the stack with additional flags or in a different context, ensure you stop the correct compose project instance.
 
 ## Mode C — Docs only
 
 ```bash
 cd docs-site
 npm run start:local
 # → http://localhost:3002
 ```
 
 ### Docs site — local search & preview
 
 This project uses a local, file-based search plugin for Docusaurus to provide a search box in the docs navbar.
 
 Quick start (dev):
 
 1. Install dependencies:
 
    ```bash
    cd docs-site
    npm ci
    ```
 
 2. Start the dev server (live reload):
 
    ```bash
    npm run start:local
    ```
 
 3. Open http://localhost:3002 and use the search box in the navbar.
 
 Preview built site (parity with production)
 
 This repository standardizes on building the docs for deployment under `/docs` (Cloudflare Pages). To preview the site exactly as production will serve it:
 
 ```bash
 # Build with DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs
 cd docs-site
 npm run build:local
 
 # Serve the built site mounted at /docs
 npm run serve:docs
 # → http://localhost:3002/docs
 ```
 
 If you prefer to preview the site at root (`/`), build with `DOCS_BASE_URL=/` and use `npm run serve` to open http://localhost:3002/.
 
 Notes
 - The plugin dependency (@cmfcmf/docusaurus-search-local) is declared in package.json and will be installed by `npm ci`.
 - The site configuration in docusaurus.config.ts will load the plugin if installed. If you build the docs inside Docker or CI, `npm ci` in the Dockerfile will ensure the plugin is available at build time.
 - If `npm ci` fails in your environment, inspect the npm logs and ensure a network/proxy is configured correctly.
 - See the Testing & Preview page for more details: [Testing & Preview](./testing-and-preview).
 
 ## Run tests
 
 ```bash
 # Frontend unit tests (Vitest + RTL)
 cd front && npm test
 
 # E2E tests (requires Mode B Docker stack running)
 cd e2e && npm test -- --project=chromium
 ```
 
 ## Environment variables
 
 | Variable | Where | Value |
 |----------|-------|-------|
 | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | `front/.env.local` | `localhost:1999` (local) |
 | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard | `sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev` (prod) |
 
 See `front/.env.local.example` for a template.
 
 > **Note:** Sessions persist in your browser's localStorage. Use the 🗑 button on the Host Dashboard to clean up old sessions.

 id: quick-start
 title: Quick Start
 sidebar_label: Quick Start

 > NOTE: Deployment and Pages instructions formerly here have moved to [Deployment & Deploy](./deployment-and-deploy). Quick Start still contains local development modes; see the canonical deployment doc for production.
 
 <!-- Partial page preserved; refer to canonical docs for deploy steps. -->
 
 
 # Quick Start
 
 Three development modes depending on what you're testing.
 
 ## Prerequisites
 
 - Node.js 20+
 - `git clone` the repo and `cd SommelierArena`
 
 ## Mode A — Fast daily dev (recommended)
 
 Best for feature work. No Docker needed.
 
 ```bash
 # Terminal 1 — PartyKit backend (port 1999)
 npx partykit dev
 ```
 
 :::tip No Cloudflare account needed for local dev
 `npx partykit dev` runs a **local in-memory simulator** — it emulates Cloudflare Durable Objects entirely on your machine with no internet connection required. Note: Cloudflare KV (`HOSTS_KV`) is not available locally; session history comes from browser localStorage only. See [Local vs Production](./local-vs-prod) for the full comparison.
 :::
 
 # Terminal 2 — Astro frontend (port 4321)
 cd front
 cp .env.local.example .env.local   # PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST=localhost:1999
 npm run dev
 ```
 
 Open `http://localhost:4321/host` (host) and `http://localhost:4321/play` (participant).
 
 ## Mode B — Full integration (Docker)
 
 Best for E2E tests and nginx/proxy validation.
 
 ```bash
 docker-compose up --build
 ```
 
 | Service | URL |
 |---------|-----|
 | Frontend (nginx) | `http://localhost:4321` |
 | PartyKit backend | `http://localhost:1999` |
 | Docs (Docusaurus) | `http://localhost:3002` |
 
 ### Docker cheat sheet
 
 ```bash
 # Start the full stack
 docker-compose up --build -d
 
 # Stop the full stack
 docker-compose down
 
 # Rebuild a single service
 docker-compose up --build -d front
 
 # View logs
 docker-compose logs -f
 ```
 
 > ⚠️ **Important:** Use `docker-compose down` to stop the running stack. If you started the stack with additional flags or in a different context, ensure you stop the correct compose project instance.
 
 ## Mode C — Docs only
 
 ```bash
 cd docs-site
 npm run start:local
 # → http://localhost:3002
 ```
 
 ### Docs site — local search & preview
 
 This project uses a local, file-based search plugin for Docusaurus to provide a search box in the docs navbar.
 
 Quick start (dev):
 
 1. Install dependencies:
 
    ```bash
    cd docs-site
    npm ci
    ```
 
 2. Start the dev server (live reload):
 
    ```bash
    npm run start:local
    ```
 
 3. Open http://localhost:3002 and use the search box in the navbar.
 
 Preview built site (parity with production)
 
 This repository standardizes on building the docs for deployment under `/docs` (Cloudflare Pages). To preview the site exactly as production will serve it:
 
 ```bash
 # Build with DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs
 cd docs-site
 npm run build:local
 
 # Serve the built site mounted at /docs
 npm run serve:docs
 # → http://localhost:3002/docs
 ```
 
 If you prefer to preview the site at root (`/`), build with `DOCS_BASE_URL=/` and use `npm run serve` to open http://localhost:3002/.
 
 Notes
 - The plugin dependency (@cmfcmf/docusaurus-search-local) is declared in package.json and will be installed by `npm ci`.
 - The site configuration in docusaurus.config.ts will load the plugin if installed. If you build the docs inside Docker or CI, `npm ci` in the Dockerfile will ensure the plugin is available at build time.
 - If `npm ci` fails in your environment, inspect the npm logs and ensure a network/proxy is configured correctly.
 - See the Testing & Preview page for more details: [Testing & Preview](./testing-and-preview).
 
 ## Run tests
 
 ```bash
 # Frontend unit tests (Vitest + RTL)
 cd front && npm test
 
 # E2E tests (requires Mode B Docker stack running)
 cd e2e && npm test -- --project=chromium
 ```
 
 ## Environment variables
 
 | Variable | Where | Value |
 |----------|-------|-------|
 | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | `front/.env.local` | `localhost:1999` (local) |
 | `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` | Cloudflare Pages dashboard | `sommelier-arena.USERNAME.partykit.dev` (prod) |
 
 See `front/.env.local.example` for a template.
 
 > **Note:** Sessions persist in your browser's localStorage. Use the 🗑 button on the Host Dashboard to clean up old sessions.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a comprehensive picture of all the docs. Let me analyze the situation and plan the cleanup:

**Current state - 30 docs files:**
1. `architecture.md` - Good, accurate, keep as-is
2. `cleanup-log.md` - Internal tracking doc, should be DELETED (not useful to readers)
3. `cloudflare-setup.md` - Deprecated + extremely long dashboard walkthrough, now marked as deprecated, pointing to `deployment-and-deploy.md`. This is VERY long (the bulk of what I read). Should be DELETED since content is now in `deployment-and-deploy.md` and `cloudflare-wrangler.md`


4. `cloudflare-wrangler.md` - Wrangler CLI guide that's solid but has some overlap with the canonical deployment doc, so I could either merge it in or keep it as a specialized reference
5. `config-comparison.md` - Useful comparison table, worth keeping
6. `data-persistence.md` - Detailed and accurate, keep it
7. `deployment-and-deploy.md` - The canonical deployment guide, keep it
8. `deployment.md` - Deprecated redirect to the canonical doc, DELETE once I verify everything's covered
9. `docs-index.json` - Machine-readable index that should stay but be hidden from navigation
10. `env.md` - Has outdated references to renamed files that need fixing
11. `event-reference.md` - Detailed and solid, keep it
12. `features.md` - Looks good but might duplicate content from `overview.md`, need to compare them
13-16. The persona pages (`for-automation.md`, `for-developers.md`, `for-partners.md`, `for-product.md`) - Short but useful, though the partners one is pretty thin
17. `gameplay-workflow.md` - Detailed and accurate

, keep it
18-21. `get-started.md`, `host-identity.md`, `intro.md`, and `local-vs-prod.md` are all solid and worth keeping
22. `networking.md` - Haven't reviewed it yet but it covers how networking functions
23. `overview.md` - Overlaps with `intro.md`, so there's some redundancy there
24-25. `prd-link-based.md` and `prd.md` - The newer one needs a full read, the older one is outdated
26. `proxy-worker.md` - Haven't looked at this one yet
27. `quick-start.md` - Marked as deprecated but still has useful local development content mixed in with deployment info

, so it shouldn't actually be removed
28-30. `tech-stack.md`, `testing-and-preview.md`, and `user-stories.md` are all good to keep
31. `validation-report.md` - This is internal only and should be deleted

Looking at the overlaps, `overview.md` and `intro.md` are doing similar work - `intro.md` is the main entry point and has more depth, so I should merge `overview.md` into it and remove the duplicate. Same thing with `features.md` and `gameplay-workflow.md` - they're covering overlapping ground on the MVP features.

I'm noticing a real discrepancy though between what `features.md` says about answer locking versus what `gameplay-workflow.md` describes, and I need to check the actual code to see which one is correct. `quick-start.md` is marked deprecated for deployment but still has valuable local dev content that shouldn't be thrown out - it just needs to be repositioned as a local development guide. `deployment.md` is fully deprecated in favor of `deployment-and-deploy.md`, so that can go. Now I'm looking at `cloudflare-setup.md` to see if it's still needed or if it's redundant with other docs.

`cloudflare-setup.md` is massive and mostly duplicates what's already in `deployment-and-deploy.md` and `cloudflare-wrangler.md`, so it should be deleted. `cloudflare-wrangler.md` has some unique CI content but otherwise overlaps with `deployment-and-deploy.md` - I could consolidate them. `cleanup-log.md` and `validation-report.md` are internal tracking documents that don't belong in the public docs. I see there are two PRD documents - `prd.md` and `prd-link-based.md` - and I haven't examined `prd.md` yet to understand if they're duplicates or serve different purposes. `for-partners.md` is pretty sparse and might be better merged into another doc.

`get-started.md` is just a link collection that could fold into `intro.md` since the root page already has a quick-links table. There's an outdated reference in `env.md` pointing to a renamed file. I also noticed `config-comparison.md` has some inaccuracies about how the Docker setup actually works - the description of Mode B doesn't match the real docker-compose configuration. Now I'm checking the `back/game.ts` file to verify some implementation details about answer mutability, available categories, and other game mechanics.

I still need to review several files: `networking.md`, `prd.md`, `proxy-worker.md`, `tech-stack.md`, `testing-and-preview.md`, and `user-stories.md`. My cleanup strategy is taking shape: I'll delete the fully deprecated files like `deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, and the internal tracking documents, then consolidate overlapping content by merging `overview.md` into `intro.md` and combining the Cloudflare-related docs.

For the files I'm keeping, I need to refactor `quick-start.md` to properly document local development instead of marking it deprecated, update `intro.md` with current information and a quick links table, fix the environment file path references in `env.md`, and decide whether to expand or merge `for-partners.md` with `for-product.md`. I should also verify the `features.md` content and resolve which PRD variant to keep.

Now I'm reviewing the remaining documentation to check accuracy against the actual codebase—particularly the timer and answer locking mechanics—and identifying which files are solid enough to keep as-is versus which ones I need to read through more carefully.

I'm noticing a conflict between `features.md` and the other docs: `features.md` claims the timer is fixed at 60 seconds for the MVP, but both `gameplay-workflow.md` and `quick-start.md` describe a configurable slider ranging from 15–120 seconds. I need to check the actual code in `back/game.ts` to see which is correct, since `features.md` might be outdated from an earlier design phase.

There's also a discrepancy on answer locking—`features.md` says answers lock on first tap and can't be changed, while `gameplay-workflow.md` says participants can modify their selection until the host reveals, with the backend accepting updates on each `submit_answer` message. The question categories seem consistent across docs though. Let me dig into the code to resolve these conflicts and build out a proper plan.

1. ---
2. id: tech-stack
3. title: "Tech Stack & Principles"
4. sidebar_label: Tech Stack
5. ---
6. 
7. # Tech Stack
8. 
9. ## Production stack (v2.0 PartyKit)
10. 
11. | Layer | Technology | Notes |
12. |-------|-----------|-------|
13. | Frontend | **Astro 5** (static) + **React 19** islands | Zero JS by default; interactivity only where needed |
14. | Styling | **Tailwind CSS v4** | Utility-first; no design system dependency |
15. | State | **Zustand** | One store each for host and participant |
16. | WebSockets | **PartySocket** (`partysocket`) | Drop-in wrapper with auto-reconnect |
17. | Backend | **PartyKit** (Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects) | One DO instance per game session |
18. | Persistence | **Durable Object storage** (SQLite-backed) | Game state survives DO eviction |
19. | Session index | **Cloudflare KV** (`SOMMELIER_HOSTS` namespace) | Maps `host:{id}` → list of session codes |
20. | DNS + TLS | **Cloudflare** (`ducatillon.net`) | Managed; zero cert renewal |
21. | Docs proxy | **Cloudflare Worker** (`proxy-worker/index.ts`) | Routes `/docs/*` to Docusaurus Pages project |
22. 
23. ## Design principles
24. 
25. 1. **No server to maintain** — Durable Objects are managed infrastructure; no VMs, no Docker in production.
26. 2. **€0/month** — Cloudflare free tier covers all traffic for a casual dinner-party app.
27. 3. **Islands architecture** — Astro renders everything at build time; React hydrated only for the game UI.
28. 4. **State machine discipline** — All business logic in `back/game.ts`. The frontend projects server state.
29. 5. **Single source of truth** — Session state is DO built-in storage. No database, no ORM.
30. 
31. ## Backend
32. 
33. `back/` — PartyKit Durable Object game logic (`back/game.ts`). This is the real-time game server.
34. 
35. ## Why PartyKit over plain Durable Objects?
36. 
37. | Concern | Plain DO | PartyKit |
38. |---------|----------|----------|
39. | WebSocket fan-out | Manual `this.state.getWebSockets()` | `this.room.broadcast()` |
40. | Local dev | `wrangler dev` (requires auth) | `npx partykit dev` (no auth) |
41. | Deploy | `wrangler deploy` | `npx partykit deploy` |
42. | Free tier | Yes | Yes |
43.

 id: tech-stack
 title: "Tech Stack & Principles"
 sidebar_label: Tech Stack
 ---
 
 # Tech Stack
 
 ## Production stack (v2.0 PartyKit)
 
 | Layer | Technology | Notes |
 |-------|-----------|-------|
 | Frontend | **Astro 5** (static) + **React 19** islands | Zero JS by default; interactivity only where needed |
 | Styling | **Tailwind CSS v4** | Utility-first; no design system dependency |
 | State | **Zustand** | One store each for host and participant |
 | WebSockets | **PartySocket** (`partysocket`) | Drop-in wrapper with auto-reconnect |
 | Backend | **PartyKit** (Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects) | One DO instance per game session |
 | Persistence | **Durable Object storage** (SQLite-backed) | Game state survives DO eviction |
 | Session index | **Cloudflare KV** (`SOMMELIER_HOSTS` namespace) | Maps `host:{id}` → list of session codes |
 | DNS + TLS | **Cloudflare** (`ducatillon.net`) | Managed; zero cert renewal |
 | Docs proxy | **Cloudflare Worker** (`proxy-worker/index.ts`) | Routes `/docs/*` to Docusaurus Pages project |
 
 ## Design principles
 
 1. **No server to maintain** — Durable Objects are managed infrastructure; no VMs, no Docker in production.
 2. **€0/month** — Cloudflare free tier covers all traffic for a casual dinner-party app.
 3. **Islands architecture** — Astro renders everything at build time; React hydrated only for the game UI.
 4. **State machine discipline** — All business logic in `back/game.ts`. The frontend projects server state.
 5. **Single source of truth** — Session state is DO built-in storage. No database, no ORM.
 
 ## Backend
 
 `back/` — PartyKit Durable Object game logic (`back/game.ts`). This is the real-time game server.
 
 ## Why PartyKit over plain Durable Objects?
 
 | Concern | Plain DO | PartyKit |
 |---------|----------|----------|
 | WebSocket fan-out | Manual `this.state.getWebSockets()` | `this.room.broadcast()` |
 | Local dev | `wrangler dev` (requires auth) | `npx partykit dev` (no auth) |
 | Deploy | `wrangler deploy` | `npx partykit deploy` |
 | Free tier | Yes | Yes |

 3. **Islands architecture** — Astro renders everything at build time; React hydrated only for the game UI.
 4. **State machine discipline** — All business logic in `back/game.ts`. The frontend projects server state.
 5. **Single source of truth** — Session state is DO built-in storage. No database, no ORM.
 
 ## Backend
 
 `back/` — PartyKit Durable Object game logic (`back/game.ts`). This is the real-time game server.
 
 ## Why PartyKit over plain Durable Objects?
 
 | Concern | Plain DO | PartyKit |
 |---------|----------|----------|
 | WebSocket fan-out | Manual `this.state.getWebSockets()` | `this.room.broadcast()` |
 | Local dev | `wrangler dev` (requires auth) | `npx partykit dev` (no auth) |
 | Deploy | `wrangler deploy` | `npx partykit deploy` |
 | Free tier | Yes | Yes |

data.content: Now I have a complete picture. Let me build the plan.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me also read `networking.md`, `proxy-worker.md`, `testing-and-preview.md`, and `user-stories.md` quickly.

Key findings so far:
1. **Answer locking**: Code says "Overwrite (no lock)" at line 542 of game.ts → `features.md` and `overview.md` say "first tap locks" - this is WRONG. `gameplay-workflow.md` is correct.
2. **Timer**: Code validates `Math.max(15, Math.min(120, timerSeconds))` and defaults to 60 → Configurable 15-120s, default 60s. `features.md` says "60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP)" - WRONG. 
3. **`features.md`**: Has multiple outdated statements from early MVP design. Needs major update.

Now I'm planning the full docs restructuring. I need to delete several deprecated files like `deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, `cleanup-log.md`, and `validation-report.md` since they're either fully deprecated or just internal tracking. I'll also merge `overview.md` and `get-started.md` into `intro.md` to consolidate the introductory content.

For the remaining files, I'm consolidating `cloudflare-wrangler.md` into `deployment-and-deploy.md` before removing it, keeping just `prd.md` as the clean link-based PRD, moving `docs-index.json` out of the sidebar as a machine-readable artifact, and deciding whether to merge `for-partners.md` into `for-product.md` or drop it entirely since it's too thin.

I also need to fix several accuracy issues: `features.md` incorrectly states the timer isn't configurable when it actually ranges from 15-120 seconds, and it's wrong about answers being locked after reveal. I'll remove the false deprecation header from `quick-start.md` and make it a proper local dev guide, then integrate the overview and get-started content into `intro.md`.

Now I'm fixing file path references in `env.md` and pulling the useful CI deployment content from `cloudflare-wrangler.md` into `deployment-and-deploy.md`. The final structure organizes everything into Introduction, Getting Started, Game Design for product users, and Reference for developers, with the Reference section including architecture, tech stack, and event documentation.

For cleanup, I'm removing the deprecated `deployment.md` and `cloudflare-setup.md`, merging `cloudflare-wrangler.md` into the canonical deployment guide, and deleting internal files like `cleanup-log.md` and `validation-report.md`.

On the persona pages, I'm reconsidering—`for-developers.md`, `for-product.md`, and `for-automation.md` are worth keeping as minimal link hubs from the intro, but `for-partners.md` is thin enough to fold into `for-product.md`. I should also merge `overview.md` and `get-started.md` into `intro.md` and keep `docs-index.json` but remove it from the sidebar.

Actually, the user emphasized wanting understandable examples for readers and contributors, so these persona pages shouldn't just be link lists—they need real substance. Here's my cleanup plan: delete `overview.md` and fold it into `intro.md`, keep `intro.md` as the root, fix `quick-start.md` by removing the false deprecation warning and preserving local dev content, update `features.md` to reflect the actual codebase instead of stale MVP constraints, remove `cloudflare-setup.md`, and merge the CI content from `cloudflare-wrangler.md` into the deployment guide.

I'm also consolidating several other files: removing standalone `deployment.md`, `cleanup-log.md`, `validation-report.md`, and `prd-link-based.md` since `prd.md` is more comprehensive, merging `for-partners.md` into `for-product.md`, and combining `get-started.md` with `intro.md`. I'll hide `docs-index.json` from the sidebar while keeping the file itself, fix the file references in `env.md`, and update `sidebars.ts` to match the new structure. Before I start executing, let me quickly scan `networking.md`, `proxy-worker.md`, `testing-and-preview.md`, and `user-stories.md` to see what they contain.

Now I'll save this plan to session state, persist it to the database, and get approval before proceeding.

1. ---
2. id: user-stories
3. title: "User Stories"
4. ---
5. 
6. # User Stories
7. 
8. ## Host
9. 
10. - I want to create a wine test by adding wines, so that each wine becomes a round with 5 questions (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name) that I fill in with a correct answer and 3 distractors.
11. - I want a generated 4-digit code for my session so participants can join easily without accounts.
12. - I want a Host Dashboard so I can control the pace of the game:
13.   - **Start** opens the lobby; starting the first round closes it.
14.   - **Pause / Resume** freezes and restores the question timer (remaining time is preserved on resume).
15.   - **Reveal Answer** manually closes the current question and highlights the correct option for all participants.
16.   - **Next** advances to the next question, or — after the last question of a wine — shows the round leaderboard and then moves to the next wine.
17.   - **End** terminates the session and pushes everyone to the final leaderboard.
18. - I want to see which participants have submitted an answer (not what they answered) so I know when everyone is done and I can reveal early.
19. 
20. ## Participant
21. 
22. - I want to join a session by entering a 4-digit code so I can participate without creating an account.
23. - I want to be assigned a generated pseudonym automatically so I don't have to think of a name.
24. - I want to answer multiple-choice questions within a 60-second window, knowing my first tap is final.
25. - I want to see the correct answer highlighted after the host reveals it, along with my points for that question.
26. - I want to see the leaderboard after all questions of each wine are completed, so I can track my standing throughout the game.
27. - I want to see a final leaderboard when the host ends the session, which stays on screen until I close the tab.
28. 
29. ## Edge cases & constraints
30.

 - I want to see which participants have submitted an answer (not what they answered) so I know when everyone is done and I can reveal early.

1. ---
2. id: networking
3. title: Networking — localhost:1999 vs the proxy URL
4. sidebar_label: Networking URLs
5. ---
6. 
7. # Networking URLs
8. 
9. Sommelier Arena exposes two WebSocket-capable endpoints depending on how you run the stack. Understanding the difference is useful when debugging or setting up a new environment.
10. 
11. ## The two URLs
12. 
13. | URL | What it is |
14. |-----|-----------|
15. | `ws://localhost:1999/parties/main/{code}` | **Direct** PartyKit dev server |
16. | `ws://localhost:4321/parties/main/{code}` | **Proxied** — browser → nginx → PartyKit |
17. 
18. Both reach the same Durable Object and the same game logic. The difference is purely in the network path.
19. 
20. ---
21. 
22. ## Mode A — Local development (no Docker)
23. 
24. ```
25. npx partykit dev --port 1999        # PartyKit listens on 1999
26. cd front && npm run dev             # Astro dev server on 4321
27. ```
28. 
29. `front/.env.local` (or `.env.local.example`) sets:
30.

 Sommelier Arena exposes two WebSocket-capable endpoints depending on how you run the stack. Understanding the difference is useful when debugging or setting up a new environment.
 
 ## The two URLs
 
 | URL | What it is |
 |-----|-----------|
 | `ws://localhost:1999/parties/main/{code}` | **Direct** PartyKit dev server |
 | `ws://localhost:4321/parties/main/{code}` | **Proxied** — browser → nginx → PartyKit |
 
 Both reach the same Durable Object and the same game logic. The difference is purely in the network path.
 
 ---

6. 
7. # Testing & Preview (Local)
8. 
9. This page documents the recommended local workflow for building and previewing the Docusaurus site as it will be deployed under /docs (Cloudflare Pages parity).
10. 
11. Recommended workflow (Option A - parity with production)
12. 
13. 1. Build the site with a /docs baseUrl (already wired in this repo's `build:local`):
14. 
15.    cd docs-site
16.    npm run build:local
17. 
18.    This creates optimized static files in `docs-site/build/` whose asset paths are absolute and start with `/docs/`.
19. 
20. 2. Preview the built site mounted at `/docs`:
21. 
22.    npm run serve:docs
23. 
24.    This runs `node scripts/serve-at-docs.js` and serves the build at `http://localhost:3002/docs` so asset paths resolve correctly and the search plugin works.
25. 
26. Notes & Alternatives
27. 
28. - If you prefer to preview at root (`/`) instead, build with `DOCS_BASE_URL=/` and use `npm run serve` (or `docusaurus serve`) to preview at `http://localhost:3002/`.
29. - Do NOT open `docs-site/build/index.html` directly with file:// when the build used `/docs` — it will show the Docusaurus "Your site did not load properly" banner because asset paths are absolute.
30.

 title: "Testing & Preview (Local)"
 audience: ""

 # Testing & Preview (Local)
 
 This page documents the recommended local workflow for building and previewing the Docusaurus site as it will be deployed under /docs (Cloudflare Pages parity).
 
 Recommended workflow (Option A - parity with production)
 
 1. Build the site with a /docs baseUrl (already wired in this repo's `build:local`):
 
    cd docs-site
    npm run build:local
 
    This creates optimized static files in `docs-site/build/` whose asset paths are absolute and start with `/docs/`.
 
 2. Preview the built site mounted at `/docs`:
 
    npm run serve:docs
 
    This runs `node scripts/serve-at-docs.js` and serves the build at `http://localhost:3002/docs` so asset paths resolve correctly and the search plugin works.
 
 Notes & Alternatives
 
 - If you prefer to preview at root (`/`) instead, build with `DOCS_BASE_URL=/` and use `npm run serve` (or `docusaurus serve`) to preview at `http://localhost:3002/`.
 - Do NOT open `docs-site/build/index.html` directly with file:// when the build used `/docs` — it will show the Docusaurus "Your site did not load properly" banner because asset paths are absolute.

2. sidebar_position: 10
3. ---
4. 
5. # Proxy Worker
6. 
7. The proxy worker is a Cloudflare Worker that sits in front of the production domain and routes traffic between the frontend and the documentation site.
8. 
9. ## What it does
10. 
11. In production, everything runs under a single domain (e.g. `https://sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net`):
12. 
13. - `your-domain.com/*` → Astro frontend (Cloudflare Pages)
14. - `your-domain.com/docs/*` → Docusaurus documentation site (separate Cloudflare Pages project)
15. 
16. The proxy worker intercepts every request. If the path starts with `/docs/`, it forwards the request to the Docusaurus Pages project. Everything else goes to the Astro Pages frontend.
17. 
18. ## Local development — proxy worker NOT needed
19. 
20. In local development, the docs and the app run as separate servers on different ports:
21. 
22. | Service | Local URL |
23. |---------|-----------|
24. | App (Mode A: Astro dev) | `http://localhost:4321` |
25. | App (Mode B: Docker) | `http://localhost:4321` |
26. | Docs (Docusaurus) | `http://localhost:3002` |
27. 
28. There is no proxy in local development. Navigate to `http://localhost:3002` to view the docs directly. The proxy is **production-only**.
29. 
30. ## Environment variable

 # Proxy Worker
 
 The proxy worker is a Cloudflare Worker that sits in front of the production domain and routes traffic between the frontend and the documentation site.
 
 ## What it does
 
 In production, everything runs under a single domain (e.g. `https://sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net`):
 
 - `your-domain.com/*` → Astro frontend (Cloudflare Pages)
 - `your-domain.com/docs/*` → Docusaurus documentation site (separate Cloudflare Pages project)
 
 The proxy worker intercepts every request. If the path starts with `/docs/`, it forwards the request to the Docusaurus Pages project. Everything else goes to the Astro Pages frontend.
 
 ## Local development — proxy worker NOT needed
 
 In local development, the docs and the app run as separate servers on different ports:
 
 | Service | Local URL |
 |---------|-----------|
 | App (Mode A: Astro dev) | `http://localhost:4321` |
 | App (Mode B: Docker) | `http://localhost:4321` |
 | Docs (Docusaurus) | `http://localhost:3002` |
 
 There is no proxy in local development. Navigate to `http://localhost:3002` to view the docs directly. The proxy is **production-only**.
 
 ## Environment variable

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me identify all issues and make a detailed plan:

**Key code facts to verify:**
1. **Answer changing** - Code (game.ts line 542): "// Overwrite (no lock)" - answers CAN be changed until reveal. `user-stories.md` says "knowing my first tap is final" and `features.md` says "first tap locks" - BOTH WRONG.
2. **Timer** - Code: configurable 15-120s, default 60s. `features.md` says "60 seconds, fixed" - WRONG (outdated MVP)

**Files to DELETE (9):**


I'm going through the documentation to identify what needs to be removed. Several files are deprecated redirects or duplicates—`deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, and `cloudflare-wrangler.md` all have overlapping content that should be consolidated. `cleanup-log.md` and `validation-report.md` are internal tracking files that don't belong in the repo. `overview.md`, `get-started.md`, and `prd-link-based.md` are redundant with better versions already in place, and there's one more file that got cut off in the list.

Now I'm looking at what needs updating across the remaining docs. `intro.md` should absorb the overview and get-started content while cleaning up duplicate quick links. Several files have factual errors I need to correct—the timer range in `features.md`, the misconception about final answers in `user-stories.md`, and a wrong file path in `env.md`. The CI deployment content from `cloudflare-wrangler.md` needs to move into `deployment-and-deploy.md`, and I need to merge the thin `for-partners.md` into `for-product.md`.

I'm also restructuring the sidebar navigation to be cleaner and more logical, grouping content into Introduction, Getting Started, For Users & Product, and Technical Reference sections. A few more files need minor fixes—`testing-and-preview.md` needs frontmatter and formatting improvements, `networking.md` has the same file path issue, and `config-comparison.md` needs accuracy tweaks. I've organized the documentation into a clear hierarchy covering configuration, deployment, and contribution guidelines. Now I'm ready to write this up as an actionable plan.

arguments.file_text:
# Plan: docs-site cleanup & consolidation

## Problem
30 docs files in docs-site/docs — many are duplicates, stale, or internal-only. Some have factual errors (timer, answer-locking). Navigation is cluttered.

## Approach
1. Verify facts against source code (`back/game.ts`)
2. Merge content where files overlap, delete the source
3. Fix factual errors found in audit
4. Restructure sidebars.ts into a clean 5-section hierarchy
5. Final build validation

## Key findings from code audit
- **Timer** is configurable 15–120 s (default 60 s) — NOT fixed 60 s as `features.md` / `overview.md` state
- **Answers** can be changed any time until host reveals (`// Overwrite (no lock)` in game.ts:542) — `user-stories.md` and `features.md` incorrectly say "first tap is final"
- `front/.env.local.example` was renamed to `front/.env.example` — `env.md` / `networking.md` / `quick-start.md` reference the old name
- `back/.env.example` was deleted — `env.md` references it

## Files to DELETE (9)
| File | Reason |
|------|--------|
| `deployment.md` | Deprecated redirect, all content now in deployment-and-deploy.md |
| `cloudflare-setup.md` | 600+ lines, deprecated, content duplicated in canonical docs |
| `cloudflare-wrangler.md` | CI YAML absorbed into deployment-and-deploy.md, then delete |
| `cleanup-log.md` | Internal tracking, not useful to readers |
| `validation-report.md` | Internal audit, not useful to readers |
| `overview.md` | Short, content merged into intro.md |
| `get-started.md` | Links-only, merged into intro.md |
| `prd-link-based.md` | `prd.md` already serves this role better |
| `for-partners.md` | Too thin, merged into for-product.md |

## Files to UPDATE
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `intro.md` | Add overview content + audience links; clean up duplicate quick-links |
| `features.md` | Fix timer (configurable 15-120s) + answer behavior (changeable until reveal) |
| `user-stories.md` | Fix "first tap is final" → can change until reveal |
| `quick-start.md` | Remove false deprecated flag; fix .env.local.example → .env.example |
| `env.md` | Fix .env.local.example → .env.example; remove reference to back/.env.example |
| `networking.md` | Fix .env.local.example → .env.example |
| `deployment-and-deploy.md` | Add CI workflow YAML section absorbed from cloudflare-wrangler.md |
| `for-product.md` | Absorb for-partners.md content |
| `testing-and-preview.md` | Add proper frontmatter (id, title) |
| `sidebars.ts` | Restructure to 5-section hierarchy |

## Target navigation structure
```
Introduction (intro.md)
Getting Started
  └── Quick Start (quick-start.md)
Game Design
  ├── Features (features.md)
  ├── Gameplay Workflow (gameplay-workflow.md)
  └── User Stories (user-stories.md)
Technical Reference
  ├── Architecture (architecture.md)
  ├── Tech Stack (tech-stack.md)
  ├── Event Reference (event-reference.md)
  ├── Data Persistence (data-persistence.md)
  ├── Host Identity (host-identity.md)
  ├── Networking (networking.md)
  ├── Proxy Worker (proxy-worker.md)
  ├── Env Variables (env.md)
  ├── Local vs Production (local-vs-prod.md)
  ├── Config Comparison (config-comparison.md)
  └── Testing & Preview (testing-and-preview.md)
Deployment
  └── Deployment Guide (deployment-and-deploy.md)
Contributing
  ├── PRD (prd.md)
  ├── For Developers (for-developers.md)
  ├── For Product & Users (for-product.md)
  └── For Automation/AI (for-automation.md)
```

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ---
2. id: overview
3. title: "Overview"
4. ---
5. 
6. # Overview
7. 
8. Sommelier Arena is a lightweight real-time quiz app for blind wine tasting with friends. A host configures a test (a list of wines) and players join with a 4-digit code. The session is played in real-time: for each wine the host reveals one question at a time, participants answer, and the host reveals the correct answer before moving on.
9. 
10. ## Core concepts
11. 
12. - **Session**: a single game instance, identified by a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`). Ephemeral — lives in memory only.
13. - **Round**: one wine. Each round consists of exactly 5 questions played back-to-back. The leaderboard is shown after all 5 questions in a round are completed, before the next wine begins.
14. - **Question**: one of the 5 fixed-category questions for a wine (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). The host pre-fills the correct answer and 3 distractors for each.
15. 
16. ## Key constraints for MVP
17. 
18. - Max simultaneous players: 10 (strict limit; lobby rejects extra joins)
19. - No mid-session joins — once the host starts, the lobby is closed
20. - Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory / client-side state only); no server-side persistence, no export/import
21. - Hosts have no persistent accounts; the host session is tied to the browser tab that created it
22. - Exactly 5 questions per wine; categories are fixed (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name)
23. - Timer per question: 60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP); resumes from where it was paused on Resume — it does not restart
24. - Scoring: fixed 100 points per correct answer; 0 points if unanswered or wrong; host cannot change point values
25. - Answer lock: first tap locks in a participant's answer — it cannot be changed

 # Overview
 
 Sommelier Arena is a lightweight real-time quiz app for blind wine tasting with friends. A host configures a test (a list of wines) and players join with a 4-digit code. The session is played in real-time: for each wine the host reveals one question at a time, participants answer, and the host reveals the correct answer before moving on.
 
 ## Core concepts
 
 - **Session**: a single game instance, identified by a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`). Ephemeral — lives in memory only.
 - **Round**: one wine. Each round consists of exactly 5 questions played back-to-back. The leaderboard is shown after all 5 questions in a round are completed, before the next wine begins.
 - **Question**: one of the 5 fixed-category questions for a wine (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). The host pre-fills the correct answer and 3 distractors for each.
 
 ## Key constraints for MVP
 
 - Max simultaneous players: 10 (strict limit; lobby rejects extra joins)
 - No mid-session joins — once the host starts, the lobby is closed
 - Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory / client-side state only); no server-side persistence, no export/import
 - Hosts have no persistent accounts; the host session is tied to the browser tab that created it
 - Exactly 5 questions per wine; categories are fixed (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name)
 - Timer per question: 60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP); resumes from where it was paused on Resume — it does not restart
 - Scoring: fixed 100 points per correct answer; 0 points if unanswered or wrong; host cannot change point values
 - Answer lock: first tap locks in a participant's answer — it cannot be changed

 - Max simultaneous players: 10 (strict limit; lobby rejects extra joins)

 - Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory / client-side state only); no server-side persistence, no export/import
 - Hosts have no persistent accounts; the host session is tied to the browser tab that created it
 - Exactly 5 questions per wine; categories are fixed (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name)
 - Timer per question: 60 seconds, fixed (immutable for MVP); resumes from where it was paused on Resume — it does not restart
 - Scoring: fixed 100 points per correct answer; 0 points if unanswered or wrong; host cannot change point values
 - Answer lock: first tap locks in a participant's answer — it cannot be changed

---
id: intro
title: Sommelier Arena Docs
sidebar_label: Introduction
slug: /
---

# Sommelier Arena

A real-time blind wine tasting quiz — Kahoot-style, €0/month to host, zero cold starts.

## How it works

A **host** creates a game session with one or more wines. Each wine gets five questions (color, country, grape variety, vintage year, wine name). Participants join via a 4-digit code on their phone, answer live as the host controls the pace, and compete on a leaderboard.

Everything runs on **Cloudflare**: the frontend is a static Astro site on Cloudflare Pages; the game backend is a PartyKit Durable Object (Cloudflare Workers); session lists are stored in Cloudflare KV.

## Core concepts

- **Session** — a single game instance, identified by a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`).
- **Round** — one wine. Each round has exactly 5 questions played back-to-back. The leaderboard is shown after all 5 questions, before the next wine begins.
- **Question** — one of the 5 fixed-category questions for a wine: `color`, `country`, `grape_variety`, `vintage_year`, `wine_name`. The host pre-fills the correct answer and 3 distractors.

## Key rules

| Rule | Value |
|------|-------|
| Max players | 10 (lobby rejects extra joins) |
| Questions per wine | 5 (fixed categories) |
| Timer per question | Configurable 15–120 s (default 60 s) |
| Scoring | 100 pts correct · 0 pts wrong/unanswered |
| Answer changing | Allowed until host reveals — no first-tap lock |
| Mid-session joins | Not allowed once first round starts |
| Persistence | DO storage (SQLite) in production; in-memory in local dev |

## What's new in v2.0 (PartyKit)

- **Zero cost, zero cold starts** — Durable Objects wake on demand
- **Session persistence** — create Monday, resume Wednesday
- **Participant rejoin** — page refresh during a game reconnects automatically
- **Host identity dashboard** — see all your past and active sessions from any device
- **Five questions per wine** — added `wine_name` category
- **Configurable timer** — 15–120 s slider at session creation
- **No-lock answers** — participants can change their answer until the host reveals
- **2 × 2 option grid** — cleaner layout on mobile

## Who are you?

| I am… | Start here |
|-------|-----------|
| A **developer** setting up locally | [Quick Start](quick-start) |
| A **developer** understanding the system | [Architecture](architecture) · [Tech Stack](tech-stack) |
| A **product person / user** | [Features](features) · [Gameplay Workflow](gameplay-workflow) |
| **Deploying** to Cloudflare | [Deployment Guide](deployment-and-deploy) |
| An **automation agent / AI** | [For Automation](for-automation) |

## Quick reference

| Doc | What it covers |
|-----|----------------|
| [Quick Start](quick-start) | Run locally in under 2 minutes |
| [Architecture](architecture) | Repo layout, runtime communication, DO lifecycle |
| [Tech Stack](tech-stack) | Stack choices and design principles |
| [Gameplay Workflow](gameplay-workflow) | Phase machine, event flow, answer/scoring rules |
| [Event Reference](event-reference) | All WebSocket message types (client ↔ server) |
| [Host Identity](host-identity) | `TANNIC-FALCON`-style IDs, session dashboard, rejoin |
| [Data Persistence](data-persistence) | DO storage keys, KV schema, what survives a restart |
| [Deployment Guide](deployment-and-deploy) | Cloudflare Pages + PartyKit + Wrangler deploy |
| [Env Variables](env) | Environment variable reference for all services |
EOF

1. ---
2. id: features
3. title: "Features (MVP)"
4. ---
5. 
6. # Features (MVP)
7. 
8. ## Host: test creation
9. 
10. - Host creates a session and adds wines (rounds). Each wine has a name and exactly 5 questions — one per fixed category: **color**, **country**, **grape variety**, **vintage year**, **wine name**.
11. - For each question the host fills in the correct answer and 3 distractor options (4 choices total).
12. - No minimum wine count is enforced, but a session with zero wines cannot be started.
13. - The session is assigned a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`) at creation time. Collisions are resolved by regenerating until a unique code is found.
14. 
15. ## Host dashboard
16. 
17. Controls available to the host during a session:
18. 
19. | Control | Behaviour |
20. |---|---|
21. | **Start** | Opens the session; participants can join via code. Lobby is closed once host starts the first round. |
22. | **Pause** | Freezes the question timer and prevents participants from submitting answers. |
23. | **Resume** | Unfreezes the timer from exactly where it was paused (remaining seconds are preserved). |
24. | **Reveal Answer** | Closes answering for the current question (if timer has not yet expired), highlights the correct option for all participants, and shows per-question score deltas. Host must trigger this manually — it does not fire automatically. |
25. | **Next** | Advances to the next question in the current round, or — after the final question's answer is revealed — shows the round leaderboard and then moves to the next wine. Disabled until the current question's answer has been revealed. |
26. | **End** | Terminates the session immediately from any state. All participants are pushed to the final leaderboard screen. |
27. 
28. The host sees a live list of which participants have answered (without revealing their choices) so they know when everyone is done.
29. 
30. ## Lobby
31. 
32. - Participants join by entering the 4-digit code and are assigned a generated pseudonym (random adjective–wine-noun pair, e.g. `TannicFalcon`).
33. - Pseudonyms are unique within a session; the app regenerates on collision.
34. - No custom names — generated pseudonyms only.
35. - The lobby rejects joins once 10 players are connected or once the first round has started, whichever comes first.
36. 
37. ## Real-time play (per question)
38. 
39. 1. Host hits **Next** (or **Start** for the first question) → question + 4 answer options are broadcast to all participants simultaneously.
40. 2. Answer options are displayed in the same fixed order for every participant (no per-player shuffling).
41. 3. Participants tap an option → **first tap locks the answer**, no changes allowed.
42. 4. The 60-second timer counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).
43. 5. Host hits **Reveal Answer** → correct option is highlighted for everyone; participants who answered correctly see +100 pts.
44. 6. Host hits **Next** → moves to the next question, or to the round leaderboard if all 5 questions are done.
45. 
46. ## Timer
47. 
48. - Fixed at 60 seconds per question.
49. - **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from the frozen value.
50. - Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
51. - Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer hits 0) are accepted if they arrive at the server before the server-side close event is processed.
52. 
53. ## Scoring & leaderboard
54. 
55. - 100 points for a correct answer, 0 for wrong or unanswered — host cannot modify these values.
56. - A **per-question score delta** is shown to participants immediately after answer reveal.
57. - A **round leaderboard** (all participants ranked by cumulative score) is shown after all 5 questions of a wine are completed, before the next round begins. The host advances past it with **Next**.
58. - A **final leaderboard** is shown to all participants when the host hits **End**. It persists on-screen until the participant closes the tab.
59. 
60. ## Connection & session lifecycle
61. 
62. - Participants who disconnect cannot rejoin — their pseudonym and score are retained in the leaderboard but they receive no further questions.
63. - If the host closes the browser tab, the session ends immediately and cannot be resumed. Participants are shown a "session ended" message.
64. - No accounts, no login — minimal friction for quick local games.
65. 
66. ## Persistence
67. 
68. - Ephemeral only: Zustand/localStorage client-side state. No server-side persistence.
69. - No session export or import for MVP.

@@ -1,69 +1,69 @@
 ---
 id: features
 title: "Features (MVP)"
 ---
 
 # Features (MVP)
 
 ## Host: test creation
 
 - Host creates a session and adds wines (rounds). Each wine has a name and exactly 5 questions — one per fixed category: **color**, **country**, **grape variety**, **vintage year**, **wine name**.
 - For each question the host fills in the correct answer and 3 distractor options (4 choices total).
 - No minimum wine count is enforced, but a session with zero wines cannot be started.
 - The session is assigned a randomly generated 4-digit numeric code (e.g. `4821`) at creation time. Collisions are resolved by regenerating until a unique code is found.
 
 ## Host dashboard
 
 Controls available to the host during a session:
 
 | Control | Behaviour |
 |---|---|
 | **Start** | Opens the session; participants can join via code. Lobby is closed once host starts the first round. |
 | **Pause** | Freezes the question timer and prevents participants from submitting answers. |
 | **Resume** | Unfreezes the timer from exactly where it was paused (remaining seconds are preserved). |
 | **Reveal Answer** | Closes answering for the current question (if timer has not yet expired), highlights the correct option for all participants, and shows per-question score deltas. Host must trigger this manually — it does not fire automatically. |
 | **Next** | Advances to the next question in the current round, or — after the final question's answer is revealed — shows the round leaderboard and then moves to the next wine. Disabled until the current question's answer has been revealed. |
 | **End** | Terminates the session immediately from any state. All participants are pushed to the final leaderboard screen. |
 
 The host sees a live list of which participants have answered (without revealing their choices) so they know when everyone is done.
 
 ## Lobby
 
 - Participants join by entering the 4-digit code and are assigned a generated pseudonym (random adjective–wine-noun pair, e.g. `TannicFalcon`).
 - Pseudonyms are unique within a session; the app regenerates on collision.
 - No custom names — generated pseudonyms only.
 - The lobby rejects joins once 10 players are connected or once the first round has started, whichever comes first.
 
 ## Real-time play (per question)
 
 1. Host hits **Next** (or **Start** for the first question) → question + 4 answer options are broadcast to all participants simultaneously.
 2. Answer options are displayed in the same fixed order for every participant (no per-player shuffling).
 3. Participants tap an option → **first tap locks the answer**, no changes allowed.
 4. The 60-second timer counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).
 5. Host hits **Reveal Answer** → correct option is highlighted for everyone; participants who answered correctly see +100 pts.
 6. Host hits **Next** → moves to the next question, or to the round leaderboard if all 5 questions are done.
 
 ## Timer
 
 - Fixed at 60 seconds per question.
 - **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from the frozen value.
 - Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
 - Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer hits 0) are accepted if they arrive at the server before the server-side close event is processed.
 
 ## Scoring & leaderboard
 
 - 100 points for a correct answer, 0 for wrong or unanswered — host cannot modify these values.
 - A **per-question score delta** is shown to participants immediately after answer reveal.
 - A **round leaderboard** (all participants ranked by cumulative score) is shown after all 5 questions of a wine are completed, before the next round begins. The host advances past it with **Next**.
 - A **final leaderboard** is shown to all participants when the host hits **End**. It persists on-screen until the participant closes the tab.
 
 ## Connection & session lifecycle
 
 - Participants who disconnect cannot rejoin — their pseudonym and score are retained in the leaderboard but they receive no further questions.
 - If the host closes the browser tab, the session ends immediately and cannot be resumed. Participants are shown a "session ended" message.
 - No accounts, no login — minimal friction for quick local games.
 
 ## Persistence
 
 - Ephemeral only: Zustand/localStorage client-side state. No server-side persistence.
 - No session export or import for MVP.

arguments.old_str:
3. Participants tap an option → **first tap locks the answer**, no changes allowed.
4. The 60-second timer counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).
arguments.new_str:
3. Participants tap an option to select it. They can **change their answer any time** until the host clicks **Reveal Answer** — there is no first-tap lock.
4. A configurable timer (15–120 s, default 60 s) counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).

arguments.old_str:
## Timer

- Fixed at 60 seconds per question.
- **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from the frozen value.
- Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
- Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer hits 0) are accepted if they arrive at the server before the server-side close event is processed.
arguments.new_str:
## Timer

- Configurable per session: 15–120 seconds (slider at session creation; default 60 s).
- **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from exactly where it was paused.
- Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
- Timer expiry fires an alarm on the server (`room.setAlarm`) — the reveal happens server-side even if the host's browser is closed.
- Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer fires) are accepted if they arrive before the server processes the alarm.

arguments.old_str:
## Persistence

- Ephemeral only: Zustand/localStorage client-side state. No server-side persistence.
- No session export or import for MVP.
arguments.new_str:
## Persistence

- **Production**: game state is stored in Durable Object storage (SQLite-backed). Sessions survive DO eviction — the host can close the browser and resume later.
- **Local dev** (`npx partykit dev`): storage is in-memory only. Restarting the dev server clears all sessions.
- No session export or import.

@@ -39,8 +39,8 @@
 
 1. Host hits **Next** (or **Start** for the first question) → question + 4 answer options are broadcast to all participants simultaneously.
 2. Answer options are displayed in the same fixed order for every participant (no per-player shuffling).
-3. Participants tap an option → **first tap locks the answer**, no changes allowed.
-4. The 60-second timer counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).
+3. Participants tap an option to select it. They can **change their answer any time** until the host clicks **Reveal Answer** — there is no first-tap lock.
+4. A configurable timer (15–120 s, default 60 s) counts down. When it reaches 0, answering closes automatically (equivalent to the host triggering Reveal Answer, but the host can also trigger it earlier).
 5. Host hits **Reveal Answer** → correct option is highlighted for everyone; participants who answered correctly see +100 pts.
 6. Host hits **Next** → moves to the next question, or to the round leaderboard if all 5 questions are done.

toolArgs.old_str:
## Timer

- Fixed at 60 seconds per question.
- **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from the frozen value.
- Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
- Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer hits 0) are accepted if they arrive at the server before the server-side close event is processed.
toolArgs.new_str:
## Timer

- Configurable per session: 15–120 seconds (slider at session creation; default 60 s).
- **Pause** freezes the countdown; **Resume** continues from exactly where it was paused.
- Participants who have not answered when the timer expires receive 0 points for that question. No penalty beyond 0.
- Timer expiry fires an alarm on the server (`room.setAlarm`) — the reveal happens server-side even if the host's browser is closed.
- Late submissions (in-flight at the exact moment the timer fires) are accepted if they arrive before the server processes the alarm.

toolArgs.old_str:
## Persistence

- Ephemeral only: Zustand/localStorage client-side state. No server-side persistence.
- No session export or import for MVP.
toolArgs.new_str:
## Persistence

- **Production**: game state is stored in Durable Object storage (SQLite-backed). Sessions survive DO eviction — the host can close the browser and resume later.
- **Local dev** (`npx partykit dev`): storage is in-memory only. Restarting the dev server clears all sessions.
- No session export or import.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
15. - Root `.env` is discouraged and not used; keep per-service examples and copy them to `.env` when developing locally.
16. - Never commit real secrets. Use CI/Platform secret stores for production values.
17. 
18. Per-service notes
19. 
20. - Frontend (front/)
21.   - Template: `front/.env.local.example` → copy to `front/.env.local` for local dev.
22.   - Key var: PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST (Mode A: localhost:1999; Mode B/Docker: localhost:4321 baked at build time)
23.   - In Docker mode the build injects the correct host; do not rely on local `.env.local` for Docker builds.
24. 
25. - Backend (back/)
26.   - Template: `back/.env.example` documents backend-only envs.
27.   - Durable Object bindings (KV, etc.) are configured in `wrangler.toml` and secrets are created via `wrangler secret put` or set in CI. Do not rely on `.env` for production DO bindings.
28. 
29. - Docs (docs-site/)
30.   - Template: `docs-site/.env.example` → copy to `docs-site/.env` to mirror production (`DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs`) or set to `/` for root serving.
31.   - The Dockerfile accepts `--build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL` to bake the base URL at build time.
32. 
33. How to use locally
34. 
35. 1. Copy the appropriate example into a `.env` (or `.env.local` for the front) in the service folder:
36. 
37.    cd front && cp .env.local.example .env.local
38.    cd back  && cp .env.example .env
39.    cd docs-site && cp .env.example .env
40. 
41. 2. Install dependencies and run the service (example for docs-site):
42. 
43.    cd docs-site
44.    npm ci
45.    npm run build:local   # uses DOCS_BASE_URL from .env
46.    npm run serve:build
47. 
48. Docker and CI
49. 
50. - Docker builds should pass necessary build args or envs explicitly; do not bake secrets into images.
51.   Example: `docker build --build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs -t sommelier-docs .`
52. - CI should set required environment variables via secrets and pass them into build jobs. Runtime envs in CI override `.env` files.
53. 
54. Troubleshooting
55. 
56. - If a local build yields incorrect base paths, ensure `DOCS_BASE_URL` is set in the service's `.env` or passed via the build command.
57. - To verify which vars are picked up, run a local script that prints key env vars (do not print secrets to logs).
58. 
59. Where to find examples
60. 
61. - front/.env.local.example
62. - back/.env.example
63. - docs-site/.env.example
64. 
65. Change log

 - Root `.env` is discouraged and not used; keep per-service examples and copy them to `.env` when developing locally.
 - Never commit real secrets. Use CI/Platform secret stores for production values.
 
 Per-service notes
 
 - Frontend (front/)
   - Template: `front/.env.local.example` → copy to `front/.env.local` for local dev.
   - Key var: PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST (Mode A: localhost:1999; Mode B/Docker: localhost:4321 baked at build time)
   - In Docker mode the build injects the correct host; do not rely on local `.env.local` for Docker builds.
 
 - Backend (back/)
   - Template: `back/.env.example` documents backend-only envs.
   - Durable Object bindings (KV, etc.) are configured in `wrangler.toml` and secrets are created via `wrangler secret put` or set in CI. Do not rely on `.env` for production DO bindings.
 
 - Docs (docs-site/)
   - Template: `docs-site/.env.example` → copy to `docs-site/.env` to mirror production (`DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs`) or set to `/` for root serving.
   - The Dockerfile accepts `--build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL` to bake the base URL at build time.
 
 How to use locally
 
 1. Copy the appropriate example into a `.env` (or `.env.local` for the front) in the service folder:
 
    cd front && cp .env.local.example .env.local
    cd back  && cp .env.example .env
    cd docs-site && cp .env.example .env
 
 2. Install dependencies and run the service (example for docs-site):
 
    cd docs-site
    npm ci
    npm run build:local   # uses DOCS_BASE_URL from .env
    npm run serve:build
 
 Docker and CI
 
 - Docker builds should pass necessary build args or envs explicitly; do not bake secrets into images.
   Example: `docker build --build-arg DOCS_BASE_URL=/docs -t sommelier-docs .`
 - CI should set required environment variables via secrets and pass them into build jobs. Runtime envs in CI override `.env` files.
 
 Troubleshooting
 
 - If a local build yields incorrect base paths, ensure `DOCS_BASE_URL` is set in the service's `.env` or passed via the build command.
 - To verify which vars are picked up, run a local script that prints key env vars (do not print secrets to logs).
 
 Where to find examples
 
 - front/.env.local.example
 - back/.env.example
 - docs-site/.env.example
 
 Change log

- Frontend (front/)
  - Template: `front/.env.example` → copy to `front/.env.local` for local dev.

- Backend (back/)
  - The backend is a PartyKit Durable Object (`back/game.ts`). It has no `.env` file — Durable Object bindings (KV, etc.) are configured in `partykit.json` and secrets are created via `wrangler secret put` or set in CI.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. --- 2. id: deployment-and-deploy 3. title: Deployment & Deploy 4. sidebar_label: Deployment 5. audience: developer 6. tags: [deployment, cloudflare, partykit, pages, wrangler] 7. --- 8. 9. # Deployment & Deploy 10. 11. This canonical document consolidates deployment and Cloudflare setup steps for Sommelier Arena. It replaces `deployment.md`, `cloudflare-setup.md`, and the deployment sections of `quick-start.md`. 12. 13. Note: Use the Wrangler CLI for reproducible, scriptable Worker deployments and Cloudflare Pages API for automation. Manual Dashboard steps are referenced for one-off tasks only. 14. 15. ## Architecture 16. 17. ```mermaid 18. flowchart LR 19. Browser -->|HTTPS| Pages[Cloudflare Pages
(Frontend)] 20. Browser -->|WSS| PartyKit[PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects)] 21. Pages --> Proxy[Proxy Worker] 22. Proxy --> Docs[Docs (Docusaurus Pages)] 23. PartyKit --> KV[Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV)] 24. PartyKit -->|Durable Object storage| SQLite[SQLite (Worker DO storage)] 25. ``` 26. 27. ## Summary of services 28. 29. - Frontend: Cloudflare Pages (front/) 30. - Backend: PartyKit (Cloudflare Workers Durable Objects) — deployed with `npx partykit deploy` 31. - Docs: Cloudflare Pages (docs-site/) 32. - Proxy Worker: Cloudflare Worker (proxy-worker/index.ts) — deploy with Wrangler 33. 34. ## Recommended automated flow (CI) 35. 36. 1. Build frontend artifact (`npm --prefix front run build`) and upload to Pages via API or Git push. 37. 2. Build docs (`npm --prefix docs-site run build`) and upload to Pages as a separate Pages project. 38. 3. After Pages projects are deployed, capture their pages.dev URLs and publish the proxy worker with `DOCS_ORIGIN` and other env vars injected via Wrangler or the Pages API. 39. 40. ## PartyKit (backend) 41. 42. Deploy: 43. 44. ```bash 45. # From repo root 46. npx partykit login # authenticate (first time) 47. npx partykit deploy 48. ``` 49. 50. Notes: 51. - PartyKit publishes Durable Objects to a project-specific domain: `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev`. 52. - Ensure `partykit.json` contains KV namespace bindings where required. 53. 54. ## Cloudflare KV (HOSTS_KV) 55. 56. Create a KV namespace and bind it to PartyKit/Worker deployments. Using Wrangler (scriptable): 57. 58. ```bash 59. # Create namespace via Wrangler (requires CF_API_TOKEN) 60. npx wrangler kv:namespace create "SOMMELIER_HOSTS" --account-id $CF_ACCOUNT_ID 61. # Wrangler prints the namespace id: add it to partykit.json bindings 62. ``` 63. 64. ## Cloudflare Pages (Frontend & Docs) 65. 66. Frontend (Pages project) 67. 68. - Root dir: `front` 69. - Build command: `npm run build` 70. - Output dir: `dist` 71. - Env: `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST` = `sommelier-arena..partykit.dev` 72. 73. Docs (Pages project) 74. 75. - Root dir: `docs-site` 76. - Build command: `npm run build` 77. - Output dir: `build` or as configured in docusaurus 78. 79. ## Proxy Worker (wrangler) 80. 81. Use Wrangler to build & publish the TypeScript proxy worker (recommended): 82. 83. ```toml 84. # wrangler.toml (example) 85. name = "sommelier-arena-proxy" 86. main = "proxy-worker/index.ts" 87. compatibility_date = "2026-03-24" 88. ``` 89. 90. Publish (example): 91. 92. ```bash 93. npx wrangler whoami 94. # Deploy with DOCS_ORIGIN injected (replace with actual pages.dev domain) 95. npx wrangler deploy --var DOCS_ORIGIN=https://sommelier-arena-docs.pages.dev 96. ``` 97. 98. To create a route for `/docs*` programmatically, use the Cloudflare REST API: 99. 100. ```bash 101. curl -X POST "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/${ZONE_ID}/workers/routes" \ 102. -H "Authorization: Bearer $CF_API_TOKEN" \ 103. -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 104. --data '{ "pattern": "sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs*", "script": "sommelier-arena-proxy" }' 105. ``` 106. 107. ## Verification 108. 109. - Visit `https://sommelier-arena.ducatillon.net/docs` and confirm the docs load. 110. - Confirm frontend loads at configured custom domain and connects to PartyKit via `PUBLIC_PARTYKIT_HOST`. 111. 112. ## Rollback 113. 114. - Cloudflare Pages: rollback via Pages → Deployments. 115. - Workers: re-deploy previous bundle via Wrangler with the previous commit. 116. 117. ## Notes & CI recommendations 118. 119. - Do not hard-code `DOCS_ORIGIN`; inject via CI or Wrangler `--var`. 120. - Store `CF_API_TOKEN`, `CF_ACCOUNT_ID`, `ZONE_ID` in GitHub Actions secrets and use them in CI steps. 121. 122. 123.
